# Thread: Hello and Thank You!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.22471/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Soleil — 2018-11-06T19:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.22471/post-255133>

Hi Everyone,

I wanted to say Hi and Thank you to the members here who share and contribute to this great forum. I’ve been a member for a few months now, but have been immersed in reading, listening and learning from Dr. Kruse for about 8 months now.

A bit about me… I spent many years in healthcare, working in a toxic nnEMF and blue light environment. Contact lenses for over 40 years. Very fair skin so would never go in the sun. My bare skin never touched the earth. I was increasingly tired, and my sleep quality was worsening every year. Tinnitus for the last 5 yrs (can trace back to smartmeter install). Body aches and pains, especially in the morning. Increasing sensitivity to EMF’s (fingers burn when touching cellphone and laptop touchpad, feeling unwell in malls/stores but fine after leaving). I’m grateful and fortunate for not having had any diagnosed disease up to this point in my life.

Changes I’ve made so far: up and out with the morning sun, grounded, regardless of weather or temp. Without fail. I’m in northern IN so will see about winter with no clothes on, but nothing deters me from being outside and grounding when the sun rises, and as often as I can during the day. Also getting outside for sunset. Protein breakfast. RO or MV spring water. No lights on in my house, not during the day, esp not at night. I get up with the sun and go to bed shortly (1-2 hrs) after dark. Easy in summer, not so much now, but I’ve adjusted my work schedule to fit. Iris for my computer. Orange glasses for nighttime, only when needed. Turned bedroom power breakers off permanently. Wifi disconnected except for minimal daytime hours. Got rid of cordless phones. Got rid of TV 6 yrs ago. Faraday cage for the Smartmeter that was installed 5 yrs ago without my knowledge or permission. No more contact lenses (over 2 yrs ago). Eating lots of seafood and foods local to my location as much as possible. No CT yet, that's next, although I’m outside, grounded, in current morning temps of 30-45 degrees (in moose hide moccasins). No fluoride for over 20 years. No sunglasses, never wore sunscreen or face makeup. No OTC or prescription meds ever.

Huge for me was understanding that it’s not a food story, but a light story. That alone has completely transformed my life. I’ve lost 18 lbs. without trying. Desire for food has completely changed/lessened. Sleep is amazing, with vivid dreams. Steady energy, brain fog lifted. No body aches or pains. NONE. The tinnitus remains. There’s much more, but these are highlights and it’s just the beginning.

Little about a Mitochondriac’s journey is initially easy or convenient. The unavoidable 5G reality dictates that I’ll need to move. Not at all what I want.. I love where I live and I love my home, but making decisions based on facts and realities, rather than what or how you’d like things to be, is required. I’m grateful to Jack for teaching N=1. Again, not easy, but it has enabled me to see life and what I need to do in a much more realistic way, the only way, really. You’re either all in, or you’re out. Thank you so much Dr. Kruse.

### caroline — 2018-11-07T05:37:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.22471/post-255149>

I love your journey to wellness Soleil.

Please start a journal in the optimal journal section so we can all follow your journey.

Where do you live?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

I've been Bulletproof for a while. A few days ago, I started putting your advice into practice just to test it and damn it works well. I've never felt better mentally. In fact, I don't even feel the need for nootropics now. Jack, thank you so much for having your info available. You're a true genius and an underrated one at that.

# Thread: Philosophy Of Science Of Medicine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-11-07T18:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255180>

Saw this link coming by on Facebook:

**[The Year’s Most Important Study Adds to Uncertainty in Science](https://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/904286?nlid=125967_1842&src=WNL_mdplsfeat_181106_mscpedit_wir&uac=129891BZ&spon=17&impID=1792456&faf=1&fbclid=IwAR07VJBqitA5fyXFLphov_zY1nCnu9aOnWqS_k3cmuCqpP9qYBZdZHcMPNA)**

The article claims that researchers in medicine, by analyzing the _same_ data, end up using very different statistical methods which lead to different results (and perhaps end up with different results by using the _same_ statistical methods).

My Facebook comments on the article were the following:

"Thanks for this article. Eye-opening.

Gives credence to my thesis that truth is always provisional from a philosophy of science perspective. Many "givens" that are blindly accepted in science today - such as "indubitable" conclusions in medicine - will probably consider half-truths or falsehoods 100 years from now.

Additionally, this article rigorously questions (or better: nail in the coffin of) the entire hypothetico-deductive model that underlies science. Why? If several different statistical methods can be used to test the same data--statistical methods which are incommensurable and which lead to different results--then I don't think logical deductions OR inductions rule this step of the scientific process. One rather ends up with an abduction (which is logically far more dangerous), as there is no straightforward method to determine whether one needs a Bayesian analysis or multivariate regression...

I could write down another 10 thoughts on the subject matter, but will end here.

Thanks  "

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-08T03:27:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255202>

Perspective colors what is believed and published.

HOW PERSPECTIVE OPERATES in SCIENCE: How you see the world defines the lens you see things through. Black Swans always take the skeptic position. Science is the culture of doubt, not belief or religion.

A. I define quackery as bypassing the scientific process and peer review to promote alternative theories to the public that are at odds with the consensus view in fields in which one is not an expert. I don't care how smart you are. If you do that, you are a quack.

B. I define quackery as obscuring the scientific process by hijacking the methodology and peer review process to promote profiteers interests. This allows continued support of alternative theories to the public that profiteers can keep selling into. These methods are at odds with the consensus view of paradigms in fields. A consensus is not part of the scientific process; science is anticonsensus. When you create science for your patron you are not an expert. I don't care how smart you think you are based upon your Ph.D. If you do that, you are a quack.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-08T03:31:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255203>

This topic interests me because we use the RCT and call it the gold standard but then......QED underpins all of nature and it rejects RCT and determinism of cause and effect because it is based on probability. One is right and the other is religion......your perspective determines which one you believe.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-08T03:56:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255207>

Today's science is as shaky as the geocentric theory of the solar system or that Santa Claus is real......but when I say something like this it seems so hyperbolic people think I am being funny........and I am not. It is true.

What is the most important question to the Black Swan: **_the pursuit of science or how science is perceived?_** Thoroughly conscious ignorance always exists before any real wisdom is obtained. Your perception frames your focus. Not only does the earth revolve around the sun but all life itself revolves around the sun and the science of light, but you'd never know this from the PEER reviewed literature. So.....how good is the evidence in a journal?? The wise learn the words "I don't know" are interchangeable with "surrender with an attitude of wonderment" and "allow the possibility”. When you become wise you always realize it is what we don't know that is most critical in resolving the present paradox in science.

Why is common sense rarely a part of the **scientific method anymore**? It seems natural to some to think that living things must be the toiling of a celestial architect. But at one time it was also common sense to think that the sun went around the earth. Overcoming naive impressions linked to common sense and have the discriminative intellect to figure out how things really work is one of humanity's highest callings. People engaged in nature realize that common sense is often a path to idiocy because knowledge unconnected to the fabric of wisdom is dangerous. It is a very counterintuitive concept to an unripe mind. Train your mind using mito-hacks to overcome this negativity bias. It is a deep deficit we humans have. Sometimes, things change in our environment and they are never the same again no matter how many times we study them. This is what calorie restriction data never will be repeated. It is why nutrition studies are rarely reproduced because the light in every study varies and is never controlled carefully. This is ironic when you consider foods are ENTIRELY MADE by sunlight, do not you think? This is why I chuckle at the food guru perspective. They are among the most dogmatic scientism gods on Earth It is also why all RCT are worthless. They have been done in worlds that varied from 0G-4.5 G and as the network changes so do the results of RCTs and we do not realize it. Technology moves the cheese in these experiments, and no one questions it. That's reality! Life moves on. And so should we……..s**_cience rarely does because they do not realize how methodology effects results and data._**

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-08T03:57:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255208>

Dynasties must fulfill their destinies. Paradigms are like dynasties. Paradigms must enforce their dynasties. Nature is different because life is nature’s dynasty. This “paradigm’ often stands at odds with manufactured dynasties. With nature, everything is hitched to everything else; It couldn't be any other way. Nature simply is, therefore nature has to be unified. Human’s are unwilling to delete dogmatic dynasties because of confirmational bias and cognitive dissonance. These biases make most people believe the paradigm’s design is correct. Modern "complexity" is based on retained isolated outdated paradigms, held together by obfuscations, rationalizations, math equations, power, prescriptions, and propaganda. The truth is always simpler than all that. That is why I teach people about nature.........

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-08T04:13:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philosophy-of-science-of-medicine.22475/post-255209>

Scientism = Orthodoxy = radicalism + time

Science is a journey, but commerce turns it into a destination. Science works by making mistakes and building off those mistakes to make new mistakes that allow us to make new discoveries. Commerce hates mistakes; mistakes involve liability and costs. Today science has been usurped by commerce and this is why science is now broken.

Theoretical roadblocks develop in people’s minds and ideas on new science because of ideology. They are only political because they feed your belief system.

Science evolves by being challenged. Not by being followed. There is no consensus in science.

Why does poor methodology in experiments hurt the public? The NIH pays for 99% of Big science and taxpayers fund the NIH. When you fund your own bad experiments you are the source of your own problems. Because it games the results for those who pay for the science. Today's science is quid pro quo. Whoever pays gets the results they seek. This is why bad science hurts us all……..it buries the good stuff from getting funded by biased data with poor controls.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mistakes-in-future-hospitals.22477/post-255185>

Why do I think this may be a bigger problem as hospitals, ERs and ORs get 5G-a-fried?

Moral of the story: make sure your surgeon has close to 20/20 vision, is not blue-light toxic and doesn't wear a bluetooth, microwave-pulsing headset during surgery. The aftermath of such an event may have required the patient to have another surgery to fix the problems of this one.

[https://www.foxnews.com/health/surg...sCIG3Wq8IPve1Nq7-1yAyTezLVsbIQoXKVK0DHmEtUZqY](https://www.foxnews.com/health/surgeon-removes-womans-kidney-during-back-surgery-after-mistaking-it-for-cancerous-tumor?fbclid=IwAR1-aXyJmTQ9LCsCIG3Wq8IPve1Nq7-1yAyTezLVsbIQoXKVK0DHmEtUZqY)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-09T00:37:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mistakes-in-future-hospitals.22477/post-255266>

Just so you all know.......it was not the spine surgeon who did this.....it was the approach surgeon who usually is a general surgeon or a vascular surgeon. This error is just not defendable at any level and the thing that gets me, is the spine surgeon should have been in the room stopping this but I bet he/she was not in the room at all.

Patients need to really do good due diligence with these surgeries. I spend a lot of time with people pre op explaining it all and 99% do not relize how important this stuff is. This is why it is important.

### Marko Pollo — 2020-01-04T01:58:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondriacs-team-up-to-save-money.22483/post-284211>

Hey mito friends,

I think we all like to spend less, many like to spend consciously, and everyone appreciates value; however I don't see much hope in negotiating discounts. What we can do is post what we think are good products or services, at a fair price, that don't leave a skidmark on the earth or rely on unfair labor practices.

I'll go first.
From Costco i like fresh dungeness crab meat. A good value at $25/lb. Caught sustainably off Oregon coast by independent small boat fisherman. I think it is better than king crab or lobster and I can prepare a couple of huge gourmet meals like omlettes, melts, bisque or crab cakes. Crab is also pricey and spoils super fast. Small fishmongers and many grocery stores will wash older crab with a dilute bleach solution rather than throw it away. Im 99.9% sure costco moves it quickly and doesn't rinse it.

Costco pork belly. Great price. No nitrates or nitrites. Probably feedlot\\ factory raised so this is far from perfect but until i can raise my own...
I cut the belly into four equal pieces, rub with redmonds kosher salt and wrap pieces in parchment paper. After a week or so in fridge i freeze three. Sometimes an hour in a cold smoker for variety. Really enjoy slicing it by hand every morning.

Buen provecho.

# Thread: Chronobiology Works - Dad's take a note for your wife and daughters

### James Lech — 2018-11-09T17:10:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chronobiology-works-dads-take-a-note-for-your-wife-and-daughters.22486/post-255321>

In an interview I did with Jack \[ [http://jameslech.co.za/2018/07/23/e...rgeon-understanding-how-nnemf-is-child-abuse/](http://jameslech.co.za/2018/07/23/ep-7-superdads-dr-jack-kruse-neurosurgeon-understanding-how-nnemf-is-child-abuse/) \]

One of the key messages was calling out BS on what I had been told by low dopamine environments. That you are good for nobody but yourself, and if you cannot be good enough for yourself, then you cannot be good enough for others.

Jack also shared his great steps of how he F\*cked himself, wife and children by his own actions and choices. An important message because by learning from our own failures, we are able to first fix ourselves before we can be an example to others.

The interview was also impactful in that God was kind enough to give me my stroke at the start of the year, and the strength to use the knowledge learned from Jack's teaching (in the interview he says how the translation of the word Surgeon, is to teach). My predicament was how do I lead by example to wife, a very hard-headed person at times, to change in the shortest time to not only help me get even better but also get herself and our daughter better, because, daughters always want to follow their mothers.

I have but one image to share:

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

\[ P.S. there was no photoshop in this image whatsoever \]

My wife did a shoot of herself with our, in a few days, 3-year-old daughter. She is nearly 7 months and our upcoming third-child, also a daughter is in her tummy. What is great that after some serious chats with my wife, and her getting a greater understanding from my wife, her motivation to embrace chronobiology was to avoid the risk fo a c-section, and avoid a shitting pregnancy filled with months of pregnancy.

I explained the pregnancy biohacks that quickly removed nausea, along by doing them with her and she did them on her own. She also understood the responsibility as a mother, that our daughter Lily will embrace and follow in her footsteps. My wife loves the grounding, sunlight, CT, changes in the of the lights in the house, computer, and blue blocking glasses. Even Lily, insists on wearing blue-blocking glasses, and when I forgot her pair at home when we went to the shops, she gave me a very big talking to and asked me to say sorry. Her vocabulary is amazing and her understanding of the science is sound.

Everyone is about the proof is in the pudding. Well, I love how seeing the sunrise every day creates a cognitive bias toward nature. It has an infectious effect on my family. Alexa (my wife, nearly 7 months pregnant) has put together a special campaign to help get women enticed into Chronobiology.

By taking all women, and giving them a special photo shoot to experience how nature changes their beauty. Having employed Chronobiology, this third pregnancy she has been her slimmest (no dieting), actually eating more, no exercise, and the specialist 4D scan showed the healthiest out of all our children, and in the doctor's practice, the specialist OBGYN asked again permission to use my wife's results at a Congress.

If you want to learn and experience Chronobiology in a different way, I recommend booking a Real Women = Every Women campaign shoot.

P.S. this shot was taken at sunset....get the idea?

Seriously ladies, and husband, if your wife feels she is too overweight for the shoot, you would be surprised what this photographer can do, with NO Photoshopping at all (my wife).

People call me odd, and often reference me to Sheldon from Big Bang Theory. But I am happy that female quantum biology presents alternative means to help women change their biology, create their cognitive bias toward nature, and thereby raise their dopamine levels.

The sum of the point of this post:
Literally but your skin in the game and into the sun because Chronobiology works.

# Thread: We cannot rely on a Device's Signal Bars for EMF Exposures
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-cannot-rely-on-a-devices-signal-bars-for-emf-exposures.22488/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2018-11-10T05:23:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-cannot-rely-on-a-devices-signal-bars-for-emf-exposures.22488/post-255375>

And here I go again breaking news over at EMF Warriors that the media dare not report. Just as I expected, people are being microwaved more than they realize, if we have a reliance on the phone signal bar-strength. This is very much like PhoneGate Scandal to me: the public has been getting misinformation about their radiation exposures in a plethora of ways. Because there are no standards, laws or FCC Regulations about the formula of connection bars actually mean and/or how they actively update, Industry continues to get away with misinformation into infinity ∞ because the public isn't aware there is a deeper health-problem at play. Public Health and Safety is literally being played.

Now, listen to what the reporter says about each carrier using a different formula. Marketers must have known about this for a decade or more: if a telecom creates a formula that shows less bars, you have more customers flee from your service. That is their bottom line, but what they don't want to let the public know is that if a person is relying on "more bars" to minimize their RF/Microwave exposure, it can be a bad move. Instead, the media shows an app about the number of nearby phone towers that are around to make a good connection (implying that when more phone towers go up and are outside someone's window, everything is magical fairies and flying, rainbow-colored unicorns for service), omitting any health implications living and sleeping next to a phone tower 24/7.

What the media will never report is that anyone can get an EMF meter and measure for oneself. This fact is always omitted on news reports because Telecoms know very well that if even 1 in 10 people carried an EMF meter on them and saw what was going on with their own eyes, it would be game over for Industry's current marketing plan. My experience has been similar in seeing higher bars than I would expect when measuring with my EMF meters; for those canaries, we already know via feel when the intensity is spiking. One final point: remember, the harder the phone is straining to keep a connection to a tower, the higher the RF/microwave emissions will be around the phone.

[https://www.abc15.com/news/let-joe-know/is-your-cellphone-lying-about-signal-strength](https://www.abc15.com/news/let-joe-know/is-your-cellphone-lying-about-signal-strength)

In time, even all the millions of useful idiots robotic-like grasping their smart phones and furiously sending/downloading trifling junk, and sitting like dumb rocks next to their Wi-Fry routers will 'get it'. Game over for telecom's wanton wireless spree.

# Thread: Hacking your Clearlight Infrared Sauna
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### James Lech — 2018-11-10T12:22:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-255379>

This is a post reply to Valuation Man \[ [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/valuation-man.19094/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/valuation-man.19094/) \]

Hi Valuation Man, I purchased one and needed to make a bunch of hacks to it.
Disabling the WiFi is easy, when you open the top, and then the silver powerbox you follow the anntennae cable and unclip it from the circuit board. The bluetooth, that is a different story but it remains dormant unless you have other bluetooth devices in the area that ping to try connect it it.

Second, it is a good idea to place a metal mesh on the seat and by your feet, then run a cable outside to give you a grounding while in the Sauna. This helped.

Third, this was an amazing diff, install UVA blacklights in the unit. You can install tubes on either side, and behind your head. If you have an electrician, he can remove the AC ballast and connect it directly to the DC circuit board of the unit, that way the flicker and MF are altered. For testing you can run a cable from the outside into the unit (thick cable)

Blue-light filter: the display screen has a super duper bright light, it is a good idea install some red film on top of it (couple of layers) else put a lowbluelights.com sticker film on it.

If you want an ozone effect, on the wooden wall panels you can purchase and install Himalayan salt rock lamps (they sell them as facade panels for saunas), once the temp hits 60C, they begin to produce ozone.

Install a wine cork to plug up the tempreature sensor. Ben Green Field also did a post on how to rebuild the top layer with insulating board in order to raise the heat further for his training.

Audio, use the stereo jack.

Vitally important, make sure you do CT immediately after to counter the excess protons generated. You can either have the ice in your bath, else build a CT DIY tank, Ben Greenfield also has some posts on it. I find the stepping stone, of bath, chest freezer, and then outside animal trough with cooling heatpump and ozonator. If you can afford a clearlight, then affording to built a good CT tank is really easy. (you can also DIY installing infrared lights in the unit but not fully needed.

Jeremy Thomley has some great pictures for the Fire Box for it.

What you will find that being in nature is easier and better. However, if you do have some rough calls and need a good boost during the winter, the above combo is helpful.

One can also do an inverse with a Hot Tub (warm water) and wearing a CoolFatBurner.com Chest and Tummy effect. Jack explains the Fornier effect to this.

### recoen — 2018-11-10T14:51:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-255383>

[@James Lech](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21577/) what does your hot tub/ cfb protocol look like? Do you wait some time between the two?

### James Lech — 2018-11-10T17:45:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-255396>

For the Sauna, directly after you go into the CT ice tub. I enjoy doing breathing hypoxia exercises and holding, hypoxia brething exercises underwater. Longest I managed was between 6-7 mins, however, don't be stupid and ensure someone is near by. Or was your question about the hot tub (hot water) and ice vests?

### recoen — 2018-11-10T17:58:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-255398>

[@James Lech](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21577/) the hot tub and ice vests. Thanks!

### John Saf — 2018-11-11T12:01:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-255433>

Great podcast. Jack talk about IR sauna hacking. He says if you cover the areas between the panels with real gold foil, it is a good IR reflector to get more red on your body
Sauna timing is also important: Not the last two hours before sleep he says.
you will find the sauna information in the second half.

[https://physics.stackexchange.com/q...gold-foil-on-equipment-and-gold-coated-visors](https://physics.stackexchange.com/questions/74412/why-does-nasa-use-gold-foil-on-equipment-and-gold-coated-visors)

### James Lech — 2018-11-15T12:25:10+0200

> [recoen said:](/goto/post?id=255398)

> [@James Lech](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21577/) the hot tub and ice vests. Thanks!

the hot tub and CT fornier effect is described by Jack in the following podcast
[https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/podcast/lifestyle-podcasts/building-a-healthy-home/](https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/podcast/lifestyle-podcasts/building-a-healthy-home/)

### Matt Justice — 2018-12-16T21:38:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-258239>

Love what you're doing sharing this here James... This might make me come off like a dick, so I'll just approach this as if I know nothing about it:

Are you sure it's a good idea to intentionally ground yourself in a carbon panel infrared sauna?

Most carbon FIR saunas usually usually do not have sufficient electric field mitigation in them, and by adding yourself to a circuit in close proximity to that, wouldn't that start leeching and allowing your body to complete the new path?

I could be wrong, but might be something very important to look into.

### James Lech — 2018-12-17T06:58:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-your-clearlight-infrared-sauna.22489/post-258294>

Good morning Matt

Thank you for the question as perhaps I didn't elaborate well enough in my original thread. Grounding into wall socket, yes, is a particularly bad idea. Especially in the place we currently stay where one get's a strong electric field reading from the grounding wire by itself out of the wall socket. Don't worry, I did make sure the live and neutral cables were disconnected completely at the source connect (physically no connection) when I made this test and scary measurement. Also the EMI, in the electrical circuit reads high voltage transients, over 800mV.

Regarding the earthing grounding within the sauna, I placed a mesh sheet on the seat and by the feet. Then I have a wire that leads out of the sauna and into a stake in the ground that is moist.

The grounding cable I installed wasn't for the sauna's fields, as the Clearlight guys built a very good shielding and circuitry system, where usual carbon FIR saunas are terrible. But I get what you mean about completing a new path. The reason I installed the cable was when I was using the sauna I would get an excess proton reaction, inflammation, but I did CT after it was fine. However, when I hacked the grounding solution I was able to be in the sauna and high temperatures for much much longer, and I didn't get the excess proton reaction.

Then when I installed the UVA blacklights as well, wow, that made an immense difference to the energy levels. I haven't installed the gold leaf yet for better IR reflection. Instead, I installed aluminum foil to better reflect the UVA light, then as a secondary, it provides better thermal insulation and IR reflection.

P.S. it is a good idea to install the UVA lights as a DC setup.

# Thread: The Modern Mythological Significance of Light, Water, and Electromagnetism
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-modern-mythological-significance-of-light-water-and-electromagnetism.22490/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Steven1 — 2018-11-10T15:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-modern-mythological-significance-of-light-water-and-electromagnetism.22490/post-255385>

I rarely post anything on here, but I have something I want to share with you. First, I’m long overdue in thanking Dr. Kruse for the positive impact he has had on my health and my life. Thank you for your tireless efforts, Dr. Kruse.

Now, what I want to share with you---I just finished releasing a fictional podcast narrative in which I integrate many of the concepts Dr. Kruse has taught me. Why do so in the form of a fictional narrative? Read on.

Humans connect through stories. Stories and myths have a long history of being used to communicate complex ideas to the masses. In the Netflix era of today, people are consuming hours upon hours of stories through an endless supply of TV shows and movies. Watching such content is generally not a productive use of time, but it is possible for stories to serve a greater purpose if they can convey meaningful information to the audience. While there are stories that do this in some ways, there aren't many examples of fictional narratives that convey health information to the audience. This is at least partly because conventional health information is too dull to include in a story that is meant to be entertaining (can you imagine a narrative structure where the grams of carbohydrate the main character eats serves as a major plot element?).

The situation is different regarding quantum biology and the message that Dr. Kruse shares with us. The essence of this message is to connect with nature to improve your health. Nature has been a plot element in stories throughout history and still is today, usually in an adversarial role (man versus nature). I think it’s time to reframe that narrative and depict nature in a positive way, so I ventured to create a story in which the relationship between nature and health serves as a major thematic element.

As I mentioned, stories often distill complex ideas into a format that is easily consumed by the masses. This is the case with many religious stories, where meaning can be derived at the surface level or on a much deeper level, depending on the individual level of analysis. In this way, stories can have a positive impact on diverse audiences (black swans and white swans alike). Certainly, quantum biology can be categorized as a difficult concept to grasp, but at the same time, the take-home message about connecting with nature is very simple. In this way, it is a good fit for a fictional narrative that allows the majority (white swans) to consume it in a basic, surface-level manner and the minority (black swans) to take a deeper dive into the science behind it.

One could argue that too much might be lost by simplifying complex ideas to fit within a fictional narrative, but I think, if done well, there is far more to be gained. Not only could it allow these ideas to reach a wider audience, there is also the counterintuitive notion that fiction is sometimes the best way to communicate the truth.

Of course, we need people like Dr. Kruse to communicate the deep science of quantum biology, but we also need artists and storytellers who can communicate that information in different ways. The more people we have coming at this from different perspectives, the more people will benefit from the wisdom of that information.

My hope is that my podcast introduces these ideas to a new audience. I also hope it serves as a proof of concept for other artists and writers whose creativity can continue to bring these ideas to the world in new ways.

If you’re interested in listening to my podcast, just search for Awake the Future in any podcast app. You can also read the book version by searching for Awake the Future on Amazon. You can access both via my website at awakethefuture.com.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Camper Trailers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eric Waldron — 2018-11-11T06:30:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/post-255431>

Does anyone have any mitochondriac-esk camper trailer nnemf hacks? I'm super interested in the idea of living minimally in an off the grid trailer, but JK says solar panels aren't the route to go, so I'd love to hear what some of you guys are doing.

### Huck — 2018-11-11T17:30:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/post-255437>

I put solar panels on my van which I use to tow my trailer. Solar panels, charge controller, inverter, and batteries are all in van. I'm also thinking about putting a 12v refrigerator in van.

Check and see where shore power comes into trailer. On many, it is right under the bed. If I had some electrician skills, I would relocate the electrical panel as far from the bed as possible.

In many trailers, the refrigerator is 3 way - propane, 12v, 110v, so you could run it on propane. Heat is usually propane. but with an electric fan. It doesn't draw many amps, so I don't know if it is much of a problem. Stove is also propane, as is hot water heater.

You could also use portable solar panels so they could be a distance from trailer, but the charge controller needs to be close to the battery. I find a 100w solar panel is enough to run lights, recharge phone, laptop, water pump, etc. A 200w panel is probably enough to compensate for cloudy weather.

My thinking is I can park the van in the sun and the trailer in the shade. I can run an extension cord from inverter in van if I need power for my ipot or air fryer (probably outside), recharging trailer batteries, etc. If in cold weather, I plan to warm up trailer and then turn off heat for night and use plenty of blankets.

### Phosphene — 2018-11-11T17:42:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/post-255438>

Interested in this as well.

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) - How do you like your custom camper? Anything you would change?

### Sun Disciple — 2018-11-11T19:33:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/post-255450>

> [Eric Waldron said:](/goto/post?id=255431)
>
> Does anyone have any mitochondriac-esk camper trailer nnemf hacks? I'm super interested in the idea of living minimally in an off the grid trailer, but JK says solar panels aren't the route to go, so I'd love to hear what some of you guys are doing.

Solar gives off a ton of DE in homes, do to the DC to AC conversion going back onto the grid. Not sure what a portable set up would be like. Whatever you do make sure you get some meters and test because your going to be in close proximity to every appliance i would imagine.

### caroline — 2018-11-12T11:12:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/camper-trailers.22492/post-255485>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=255438)
>
> Interested in this as well.
>
> [@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) - How do you like your custom camper? Anything you would change?

That is such a loaded question for me! I loved our camper trailer because you sleep under canvas and the kitchen is outside - so you spend all your time outside.

Our new slide on camper is more like glamping....and we haven't had it long enuf or been away long enuf to get a better routine going.

So far - I tend to cook inside because it is faster than dragging out the BBQ and all the accoutrements....and I have a great fridge and sink and a ton of storage inside.

We are going away tomorrow for 6 days to a music festival so we are going to set up everything outside and I will get in some kind of a routine if it kills me! The trouble is when you love good food and prepare everything from scratch - it takes a lot of thought .

It is going to be BBQ steak and salad and a roast leg of lamb this week. We have a huge thick steak and a big leg of lamb so lots of leftovers.

But .....sleeping under canvas rocks!

We have talked to loads of campers ....and everyone agrees - there is no perfect camper.

We are outside every morning for sunrise and as much of the day as humanly possible.

We meet tons of people who are permanently on the road and loving it. It is a very appealing lifestyle ...for sure in this country.

We spent 6 weeks in North Queensland during our winter ........and that was fantastic. Except for the crazy price of diesel in this country. In our town it is presently $1.71 a litre. When we are travelling it is costing us almost a $1000.00 a week.

Everything is a trade off........

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-11-12T21:40:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-i-find-a-cell-tower-map.22496/post-255518>

I suspect the problem is either at your web browser or your network provider (ISP). A browser Internet error message that just says 'Sorry' and fails to connect to web site is something I haven't encountered.

# Thread: Breast health questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-health-questions.22498/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jen N — 2018-11-13T01:48:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-health-questions.22498/post-255526>

I'm mostly read and learn from this forum and I'm grateful for all the expertise, so I figured you all would be able to help me with questions about breast health.

Here's a bit of my health history
Age 43
No children yet, actively trying to conceive, 3 chemical pregnancy's this year so getting closer!
History of birth control bill (age 16-25) and IVF in my early 30's before I knew better!
Diagnosed with late stage Lyme disease and co-infections in 2012
I've been healthy and free from the infections listed above since 2014
Paternal aunt had breast and lung cancer, she was also a smoker, worked night shift & had a terrible diet
Been following Dr Kruse's advice regarding LWM, diet, etc since 2015

In Nov. 2016 I had what I believe was an inflamed milk duct that caused moderate swelling in my left breast that came on suddenly. I could feel a "hard spot" and it subsided within 72 hours after I used massage plus comfrey and castor oil topically. I had my first thermogram a couple weeks later to establish a baseline and have been using that to track any changes.

My OB/Gyn also suggested I have an ultrasound to check it what may have caused the swelling but I couldn't find any doctor willing to do only an ultrasound, so I was forced into a mammogram. Of course they "saw something" on both breasts during mammogram but it was not in the same area as the swelling occurred and then that led to a recommendation of a biopsy on my left breast. The biopsy came back negative.

Since 2016 my OB wanted me to have a mammogram every 6 months, I told her I wasn't doing that and asked for ultrasound monitoring instead since I have dense breasts. During the ultrasounds they have been tracking an area of micro calcifications in my right breast. They have not been concerned about my left breast, which was originally what flared up.

Today, I had a follow up ultrasound and the radiologist said the area has increased in size slightly (they never tell me the actual size!) and there is now vascularity so they want to do a needle biopsy on the area. I should note that I am in the luteal phase of my cycle and could be pregnant. I honestly do not want to do the biopsy!

Two weeks ago, my follow up thermogram said this "there is increasing activity in both breasts since the previous study, especially in both upper breasts. This is non-specific, and may be due to fibrocystic/hormonal variation, though more serious developing pathology is not excluded. Further close interval follow-up is warranted." During the thermogram, I was on cycle day 1.

I would like to hear from other more educated folks on breast health, on what to really be concerned about, and if I should have the biopsy. What I've read about calcifications is that they are often due to magnesium deficiencies, I've been working on increasing magnesium. I'm also open to reading any books/blogs on the topic breast health from a holistic perspective.

Thank you!

Jen


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Sun with Carbs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cap — 2018-11-13T02:11:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-255527>

could anyone here link good research showing

1\. the improvement of Carb metabolism/partitioning through sun exposure?
2\. The sun burning calories

Thank you

### drezy — 2018-11-13T16:44:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-255554>

> [cap said:](/goto/post?id=255527)
>
> could anyone here link good research showing
>
> 1\. the improvement of Carb metabolism/partitioning through sun exposure?
> 2\. The sun burning calories
>
>
> Thank you

How is this for a start?

"A study in the September 2006 issue of _Progress in Biophysics and Molecular Biology_ demonstrated that in young and elderly adults, serum 25(OH)D was inversely correlated with blood glucose concentrations and insulin resistance. Some studies have demonstrated high prevalence of low vitamin D levels in people with type 2 diabetes, although it is not clear whether this is a cause of the disease or an effect of another causative factor—for example, lower levels of physical activity (in this case, outdoor activity in particular)."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)

### cap — 2018-11-13T17:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-255555>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=255554)
>
> How is this for a start?
>
> "A study in the September 2006 issue of _Progress in Biophysics and Molecular Biology_ demonstrated that in young and elderly adults, serum 25(OH)D was inversely correlated with blood glucose concentrations and insulin resistance. Some studies have demonstrated high prevalence of low vitamin D levels in people with type 2 diabetes, although it is not clear whether this is a cause of the disease or an effect of another causative factor—for example, lower levels of physical activity (in this case, outdoor activity in particular)."
>
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)

Thank you for the response drezy. It's interesting but it doesn't show whether low levels of sun exposure or (low levels of vitamin D in this case) are the "cause or an effect of another causative factor"...basically this could be just from not exercising enough. I also am particularly looking for sun exposure not just vitamin D levels as that could be supplemented and by default low vitamin D levels will create an unhealthy environment, I don't think anyone is debating that

### drezy — 2018-11-13T18:05:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-255561>

I think your closest link will end up in the insulin & leptin corner:

"We observed that chronic exposure to UVR suppressed weight gain and the development of glucose intolerance, insulin resistance and signs of NAFLD in C57BL/6J male mice fed a high fat diet"

"Ongoing artificial sunlight (UVR) “ameliorated hepatocyte apoptosis, inflammation, fibrosis, and insulin/leptin resistance” when administered for 12 h a day for 6–12 weeks to rats compared to untreated controls"

from:
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5086738/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5086738/)

Enough keyword searches and you'll probably be able to scrape something more substantial together along with come of the references of the article.

Anecdotally, sun exposure helps me reduce the amount I eat moreso than burning calories as far as I can tell.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-255740>

>
> "We observed that chronic exposure to UVR suppressed weight gain and the development of glucose intolerance, insulin resistance and signs of NAFLD in C57BL/6J male mice fed a high fat diet"
>
> "Ongoing artificial sunlight (UVR) “ameliorated hepatocyte apoptosis, inflammation, fibrosis, and insulin/leptin resistance” when administered for 12 h a day for 6–12 weeks to rats compared to untreated controls"
>
>
> from:
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5086738/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5086738/)
>
> Enough keyword searches and you'll probably be able to scrape something more substantial together along with come of the references of the article.
>
> Anecdotally, sun exposure helps me reduce the amount I eat moreso than burning calories as far as I can tell.

Ithnx drezy, 'll keep looking. was hoping to find something on humans.

### victor_dan — 2018-11-28T14:37:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-with-carbs.22499/post-256835>

Hmmm....... **Sun does not burn calories in any way!**
Heat makes the body cool down so there is no carb-consuming process..
Sun exposure is great to produce Vitamin D if you eat milk products. I think it's enough to expose your hands for 30 minutes a day. So Calcium metabolizes properly.
The heat from the Sun will make your body stock more fatty layers because: the temporary glucose deposit (liver) will not consume on its own.

# Thread: Dr Kruse, newbee here needing help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/>

**Posts:** 46

### Cheryl Cole — 2018-11-13T16:01:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-255548>

Hi, I have been following this site for a few weeks, read the book and must have listened to almost all pod cast/youtube videos. My issue is, I had gastric bypass 10 years ago, my weight has been stable but wants to lose a few lbs so I started a Keto diet Aug 1, 2018, lost 20 lbs and everything was fine till 3-4 weeks ago my hair started falling out like crazy, I mean a lot of hair. Luckily I am blessed with a full head of hair but at this rate it as gotten very thin. I have thyroid issues and I'm on BHRT. My normal annual blood work is in Jan but since this is happening I think I will make an appt ASAP, what should I be getting tested??
I currently take daily 227.5mcg Nature Throid, .75mcg Cytomel, 1mcg Estradiol, 150 mcg SR Progesterone, and 200mg/ml Testosterone Cypionate injections weekly. My blood test for thyroid it very high but in order for me to feel right I can't lower it. I have started implementing some of Dr K's ideas, look at the sunrise in morning, get out between 8-10 and then again mid day. I have blueblockers on all day inside at work, and at home., eating seafood at a mim of 1 a day, trying for twice, and taking 2 fish oil pills a day, started CT last night. I want to fix my hormones and I haven't see much for a gastric bypass patient do anyone's help would be appreciated.

Hi Cheryl and welcome. Please start a journal in the optimal journal section and give Dr. K. your health history and also your Mom's and Gran's too.

Where do you live? work? your post code matters.

How is your sleep? libido?

What water are you drinking?

Have you had a 23&me test done?

Have you watched Jack's 3 Nourish Vermont talks?

If you become a gold member you will get all of Jack's monthly blogs and the follow up live Q&A. There is a thread about the October Q&A - have a read.

Jack has written about gastric bypass ......

### JanSz — 2018-11-15T17:55:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-255680>

> [Cheryl Cole said:](/goto/post?id=255667)
>
> I looked at the compound pharmacy receipt again, does 0.5ml sound right? I had a cream in the beginning but my skin stopped taking it so I switched to the injections and the cream was 0.45

If you are doing weekly injections of 0.5cc=0.5ml
Of 200mg/ml testosterone cypionate
Then you are injecting 100mg testosterone once a week.
That would be good exploratory dose for man.
But you are woman.
Women need about 10x less testosterone than man.
Also once weekly injections may cause too large variaions.
Twice weekly or EOD work better.

You could learn a lot about your Steroid Hormone PANEL from
Dutchtest.com
Test.

### JanSz — 2018-11-15T18:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-255681>

Compounded testosterone creams are whole another world.
Some work some do not,
Depending on it base.
Testing is a must.
Person who prescribe it must be willing to listen to pharmacists suggestions.
Patient must be willing to test frequently.

### JanSz — 2018-11-15T21:31:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-255705>

> [Cheryl Cole said:](/goto/post?id=255691)
>
> Thanks JannSz, I think all my hormones are out of wack at this point but I made the appt to see my OBGYN 12/11/2018 and we will do a full hormone panel and see what we get. The "full" hormone Panel by your OBGYN may be ok to monitor dosing, but it will open you to lots of guessing.

Show your OBGYN this test but first make sure that he/she sits.
Print out this sample test so you have it with you for discussions.
[https://dutchtest.com/product/dutch-plus/](https://dutchtest.com/product/dutch-plus/)

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:17:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263444>

**KREB'S BICYCLE WISDOM:** You should never rely on thirst to dictate water consumption because it lags the real effect. Calculating your total body water deficit is another way to try to measure how badly your engine (TCA/urea cycle) is working. Total body water is a function of metabolic rate and state of the mitochondrial matrix. We covered this in the April 2013 webinar. By the time thirst kicks in, your serum osmolarity is already impaired. Dehydration is a major cause of daytime fatigue as well, and dehydration slows your metabolism by 2-3%. As time goes on this imbalance can steepen dramatically in a person in an altered environment. In fact, just a 2% drop in total body water can cause neurologic changes to show up. How do I know this? I am a neurosurgeon, and we see these swings all the time in trauma cases and brain tumors that are associated with syndromes called SIADH, cerebral salt wasting syndrome, and diabetes insipidus. They are related to vasopressin which is released from the POSTERIOR PITUITARY. Now think about what I mentioned in the April 2013 webinar for our members. Are you beginning to connect any dots that dehydration and nnEMF may be linked? Could this be why modern humans are afflicted by the opiate crisis? Look at the picture below. To understand how we work you need to see how the pieces fit and affect one another in a nonlinear fashion.

Beta-endorphin is made via POMC and solar exposure of the eye as I showed in my Vermont 2017 video on Youtube. Morphine is an exogenous opioid that is used for people in pain. People deficient in solar exposure in the IRA, UVA, and UVB ranges have more pain and require more opioid drugs than those who get outside with the skin and eyes exposed to sunlight more often. THIS IS WHY IN THE LITERATURE THOSE WITH LOW VITAMIN D 25 DOH ALWAYS STRUGGLE WITH PAIN SYNDROMES. THE VITAMIN D does not cause the pain, but it is a proxy that the DRUG USE IS DUE TO BEING INDOORS around blue light and nnEMF WAY that causes the INTRACELLULAR DEHYDRATION because of the mitochondrial defects in water production at CCO.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:23:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263445>

We learned in RATS in the early 1990s that the circadian profile of arginine-vasopressin (AVP), a major peptide in the dorsomedial SCN, in rats varies. How does it vary in the nocturnal mammal? Under light-dark (LD), constant dark (DD) and constant light (LL) conditions. Under LD conditions, AVP levels in the SCN **_showed circadian rhythmicity with a peak at early light phase and a broad trough during the dark phase._**

This rhythm in the AVP contents was maintained even after 14 days of free-running under DD conditions and 3 days under LL conditions. These circadian patterns of AVP are similar to those of somatostatin, another peptide in the dorsomedial SCN. **This data indicated back to us in 1992 there HAS TO BE a common mode of regulation for peptides in this subfield of the SCN.

Guess what that common mode was? in 2014 where did we find melanopsin? BLOOD VESSELS. Blood is 93% water......normally, right?**

Vasopressin (arginine vasopressin, AVP; antidiuretic hormone, ADH) is a peptide hormone formed in the [hypothalamus](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP008), then transported via axons to the posterior pituitary, which releases it into the blood.

AVP has two principle sites of action: the kidney and blood vessels where MELANOPSIN RESIDES.

1.  The primary function of AVP in the body is to regulate extracellular fluid volume by regulating renal handling of water, although it is also a vasoconstrictor and pressor agent (hence, the name "vasopressin"). AVP acts on renal collecting ducts via V2 receptors to increase water permeability (cAMP-dependent mechanism), which leads to decreased urine formation (hence, the antidiuretic action of "antidiuretic hormone"). This increases blood volume, cardiac output and arterial pressure.
2.  A secondary function of AVP is vasoconstriction. AVP binds to V1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle to cause vasoconstriction through the [IP3 signal transduction pathway](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP011b) and Rho-kinase pathway, which increases arterial pressure; however, the normal physiological concentrations of AVP are below its vasoactive range. Studies have shown, nevertheless, that in severe hypovolemic shock, when AVP release is very high, AVP does contribute to the compensatory increase in [systemic vascular resistance](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP021).

There are several mechanisms regulating the release of AVP, the most important of which are the following:

1.  Hypovolemia, as occurs during hemorrhage and dehydration, results in a decrease in atrial pressure. Specialized stretch receptors within the atrial walls and large veins (**cardiopulmonary baroreceptors**) entering the atria decrease their firing rate when there is a fall in atrial pressure. Afferent nerve fibers from these receptors synapse within the [nucleus tractus solitarius](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP008) of the medulla, which sends fibers to the hypothalamus, a region of the brain that controls AVP release by the pituitary. Atrial receptor firing normally inhibits the release of AVP by the posterior pituitary. With hypovolemia or decreased central venous pressure, the decreased firing of atrial stretch receptors leads to an increase in AVP release.
2.  Hypotension, which decreases arterial [baroreceptor firing](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP012), leads to enhanced sympathetic activity that increases AVP release.
3.  Hypothalamic osmoreceptors sense extracellular osmolarity and stimulate AVP release when osmolarity rises, as occurs with dehydration.
4.  [Angiotensin II](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Pressure/BP015) receptors located in a region of the hypothalamus regulate AVP release – an increase in angiotensin II simulates AVP release.

[Heart failure](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Heart%20Failure/HF003) is associated with what might be viewed as a paradoxical increase in AVP. Increased blood volume and atrial pressure associated with heart failure should decrease AVP secretion, but it does not. It may be that sympathetic and renin-angiotensin system activation in heart failure override the volume and low-pressure cardiovascular receptors (as well as the hypothalamic control of AVP release) and cause an increase in AVP secretion. Nevertheless, this increase in AVP during heart failure may contribute to the increase in systemic vascular resistance as well as the enhanced renal retention of fluid that accompanies heart failure.

AVP infusion is sometimes used in treating septic shock, a condition that can be caused by a bacterial infection in the blood and the release of bacterial endotoxins such as lipopolysaccharide. Infusion of AVP increases systemic vascular resistance and thereby elevates arterial pressure. Some studies have shown that low-dose infusions AVP (which are used in septic shock) also cause cerebral, pulmonary and renal dilation (mediated by the endothelial release of [nitric oxide](https://www.cvphysiology.com/Blood%20Flow/BF011)) while constricting other vascular beds.

CITES:

Den Ouden, DT and Meinders, AE. Vasopressin: physiology and clinical use in patients with vasodilatory shock: a review. The Netherlands Journal of Medicine 63:4-13, 2005

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:29:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263446>

At the core of the mammalian circadian oscillator lies an intricate arrangement of feedback loops..........and if you listened to last night's Q & A carefully for the February 2019 webinar you would have heard me say to Luke that when you do anything to introduce an exogenous material we MAKE......you might be creating collateral damage because you have ruined feedback control mechanisms you really do not understand.

So what does vasopressin control in humans?

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:30:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263447>

Eric Trudel and Charles Bourque at the Research Institute of the McGill University Health Centre in Montreal, Canada, propose a mechanism by which the body's circadian system, or internal clock, controls water regulation.

CITES:

1.  Trudel, E. & Bourque, C. W. Nature Neurosci. doi:10.1038/nn.2503 (2010).

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:32:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263449>

Trudel and Bourque tested the idea that lower clock-neuron activity might allow osmosensory neurons to more easily activate vasopressin-releasing neurons, which would mean more water retention and less urine production during sleep.

To do this, they isolated thin slices of rat brain containing intact sensory, vasopressin-releasing and clock neurons. Even when removed from the brain, clock neurons continue to mark time.

The duo then stimulated the sensory neurons and recorded any electrical activity in the vasopressin-releasing neurons to monitor communication between the two cell groups. The researchers then moved on to look at the effect of the clock cells on this pathway. When they did not activate the clock cells during the 'sleep' part of their cycle, it was easier for the sensory cells to communicate with vasopressin-releasing cells. Conversely, when they activated the clock cells, this communication decreased markedly.

The results suggest that clock cells function as a dimmer switch for water control. When their activity is high, they prevent sensory cells from instructing secretory cells to release vasopressin. Then, when clock cells are less active, sensory cells can easily instruct secretory cells to release vasopressin, ensuring that the body holds on to its water reserves.

This tells us when SOLAR redox is low you lose circadian control of water balance

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:33:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263450>

LARGE CAVEAT: The cited study above was done in rats, which are nocturnal. Although the vasopressin cycle and clock-neuron activity are similar in rats and humans, the question of whether the same mechanism occurs in animals that sleep at night STILL remains to be answered properly to my satisfaction.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:48:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-newbee-here-needing-help.22500/post-263451>

One of his key goals is to design a sensitive vasopressin test for healthcare workers to use for people with circadian arrhythmia in the ER. If levels can be assessed rapidly, it could help us, doctors, to understand how the body is managing water, giving clues as to how to deal with various illnesses with blue light and nnEMF exposures.

Until recently (2017), it was thought to only work in a classic, **negative feedback loop**: when we are dehydrated (**bad matrix function at CCO**), levels of vasopressin rise, which causes urine to become concentrated in the kidneys, freeing up more water to be used in the body. Conversely, _when we have too much water in our body, vasopressin levels decrease, and urine is diluted. What is that is not all that it does?

What if drinking water post-sunset is mandatory for the outdoor living creature?

_
The Canadian research showed that [mice increased water intake](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v537/n7622/full/nature19756.html) just before sleep. Rather than being motivated by a physiological need for water, the drinking response was solely based on the animal's circadian rhythms. **<----------BIG FREAKING DEAL.**

In 2017 the same researchers found Vasopressin does more than they thought initially. They found that not only is there a vasopressin circadian feedback loop, but "it's also involved in feed-forward mechanisms. This is quite important in understanding from a chronobiologic perspective. They determined that this molecule is produced in the brain right before people go to bed WHEN IT IS SUPPOSED TO BE DARK, and during sleep, in anticipation of the _**dehydrating effect of sleep**_. See during sleep autophagy is very active if you are healthy.......and when you are recycling mitochondria during autophagy you cannot make any water at CCO. What happens if you cannot make water even in the sunlight because you've lost total control of autophagy because of chronic toxic blue light and nnEMF light stress? There is only one way to repair that well. It means that we need SUNLIGHT IS MANDATORY to make water at CCO during the day. If you do not get enough or live at a high latitude and inside you need more water. If we do not get enough sunlight then we lose circadian feedback control of vasopressin and the entire water cycle in our body. So.......Dr. Boros has not thought this out well from the Black Swan perspective.

# Thread: My Day with Dr Jack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-day-with-dr-jack.22505/>

**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Green Eyes — 2018-11-15T01:16:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-day-with-dr-jack.22505/post-255634>

I had the opportunity today to meet Dr Jack Kruse. He was giving a talk in Cancun to Eye Care providers ( my understanding) and offered private consults to members of the Optimal Website. I jumped at the chance. Being a relative newbie I was a little bit nervous. My significant other, Pete came with me to the hotel. After all, I told him after our " spur of the moment" trip to Cancun, Mexico, that I was going to meet some guy named Jack on the beach at Cabana #5. I got to hand it to him, he was a good sport and even agreed to not wear his Sunglasses for the day.

After some rearrangement of plans, which I was to learn later can be typical of Dr Jack flowing with the Universe, I met the big guy in my bathing suit under a clear blue sky on one of the most sunshineyest days I have seen in a long time. We did my hour long private consult in the swimming pool drinking mineral water. Afterwards, Dr Jack graciously invited us back to his infamous double decker Cabana #5, and introduced us to the other black swans who came to soak up the Sun and learn more about all this Sun, magnetism, and water stuff.

Dr Jack was kind, welcoming, and down to Earth. He doesn't beat around the bush, and will give it to you honestly. He is sincerely passionate in his desire to give you the information you need to improve your life and health. The effort and energy to make the improvements must come from you.

What did I learn from my hour long consult in the Mexican Sun? You can't plant an Orange tree and expect it to give you oranges, if you cover it up with a tarp......no matter how much food and water you give it. YOU GOT TO GET THE SUN IN YOUR EYES. What am I going to do when I get back home to Wisconsin? I'm going to change my job so I can get 1 hour of Sunshine every morning, even when it's 10 degrees outside. Next, we're selling the house and moving South. And Pete? He agreed not to wear Sunglasses in the day and to turn off the lights at Night. On the whole, this trip was a great big WIN-WIN for the both of us. Thank you Dr Kruse. Your Optimal in my book.

He isn't scary at all, or harsh, or judgemental.

But ....you do need to have skin in the game. If you tend to say that you can't do something - this isn't the place for you.

Jack gives us at least 150% ....we need to do the same.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-23T17:55:25+0200

[https://www.facebook.com/permalink.php?story\_fbid=307923729816350&id=290663984875658&\_\_tn\_\_=K-R](https://www.facebook.com/permalink.php?story_fbid=307923729816350&id=290663984875658&__tn__=K-R)

### Green Eyes — 2018-11-24T17:44:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-day-with-dr-jack.22505/post-256447>

Ok, my Optimal journey update. I'm a basic newbie beginner trying to understand this big complex stuff. Since my consult with Jack, I have:
1) given notice at work (hospital, no windows, work 12 hrs day) that I am cutting my hours in half.
2) got some very nice RA OPTICS blue blockers to wear for myself and my husband. There on sale for 25% off now. Thank you Matt M.
3) Started walking at Sunrise for an hour. Beats sitting in the Cold.

Notice there are many questions about how to get sunshine when your living up North in the Winter. To me, there's a difference between sunshine and sunlight. I'm thinking that even grey cloudy morning light is better than sitting inside during the Winter. Wondering if that's correct?

### Inger — 2018-11-24T17:58:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-day-with-dr-jack.22505/post-256451>

Absolutely! Grey and cloudy is awesome for being outside too, and a must, if you want to feel good in winter. There comes a lot of light through the clouds too, and it is whole spectrum, un-polarized, as it should be   Living waves of information

I have to admit I am a little lazy in the winter mornings, so I often just lay in my cozy bed by the wide open window and watch the sky as it get lighter...lol
I do get that light directly on my eyes and face though and it feels so good, even if rainy and grey
I often think, this is how sick people should have their beds at the hospital! Right by the wide open window, so they could watch the sky, the birds and the trees with nothing in between

# Thread: Pursuit Of Happiness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pursuit-of-happiness.22507/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Safeby2 — 2018-11-15T04:23:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pursuit-of-happiness.22507/post-255648>

Hello there fellow mitrochondriacs! My name is Samuel. Im 25 years old residing in Gainesville, Florida. A beautiful place to get sun!

Ive been lurking the forums anon for a while as I am not much of a poster on forums. However this may be a place that I might be sticking around. Regardless I will forever be following Jack's guidelines. Let me get you up to speed with me.

Since I went on lowfat diet at 18 to lose weight I have never felt right. Well I never felt right ever since I had 4 amalgams in my baby teeth and grew up on a SAD diet. I always had OCD, intrusive thoughts, depression, and anxiety. At 18 I was bed ridden due to my weight loss plan I subscribed to. Ever since Ive always had issues with my mental health in regards to feeling alert and alive. I have always been hiding behind a curtain of brain fog. Until I read up on Jack Kruse.

Now I still have fog, but its getting better. I am eating A LOT of seafood, about 5 oysters a day, .25-.5 lbs of fish a day(usually steelhead trout), and around 1.5 lbs of beef. Im mainly zero carb, but I do eat fruits every few days. Pineapple or melons are my go to.

I've noticed I dont get much brain fog from seafood, but after eating beef it happens. I want to make a journal and report back how I am doing in the future. I'm really on a quest to be 100% mindfully content and happy with life. I strive to feel the same clarity and wellbeing I used to feel as a pot smoker. I have since stopped over the past month, I do still consume coffee but thats due to me working 3 jobs. I rise and rest with the sun as much as possible and get to check out the sets. My brain is feeling better as days go on, but it still gets dull sometimes.

Sorry if this is all over the place, I have my laptop hardwired to my internet and I am trying to make this post as I get my cold bath ready for some CT! Today I learned how to disable the wifi on my router so it is currently off. I noticed a difference almost immediatly. As I did when I first put my phone on airplane mode.

I want to add that oysters are dangerous! Last night with an oyster shucker I stabbed the knife all the way through my thumb. Was more shocked the fact that I did than the fact that there was fatty tissue now hanging out of my thumb.

Carry on my fellow Mitoluvers, I'll talk to you all soon hopefully!

Take care

Samuel

### drezy — 2018-11-15T15:15:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pursuit-of-happiness.22507/post-255661>

> [Safeby2 said:](/goto/post?id=255648)
>
> Today I learned how to disable the wifi on my router so it is currently off. I noticed a difference almost immediatly. As I did when I first put my phone on airplane mode.

Nice progress. It's tuff like this learning and implementing that will give your fellow optimizers here the signal that you crave enough to learn and act on what you learn. It's way more enjoyable to share with people who act in this way. Keep it up.

> I want to add that oysters are dangerous! Last night with an oyster shucker I stabbed the knife all the way through my thumb. Was more shocked the fact that I did than the fact that there was fatty tissue now hanging out of my thumb.

A little horizontal gene transfer now and then is alright. Just walk it off. At 25 you'll be healed before the next unfunny meme gets created.
