Im just stumped. Ive been doing everything I possibly can at the moment(other than moving and thats entirely not possible for right now). I know that i shouldnt worry about mercury in seafood but im afraid sometimes i may be hurting myself while benefiting.

I need to get off coffee, which I did today and noticed better digestion in regards to brain fog, but its still there. I should also add that i dont consume any added salt in my diet as when I used to it made my symptoms way worse. I do eat entirely raw and mainly animal based, which from my research and intuition, that salt doesnt seem necessary on a raw animal based diet. From some hardcore raw paleo diet people they say it makes them feel bad. To me everything tastes over salted and Im left with an extreme rush of what feels like misfiring in my brain.

Tomorrow I am planning on watching the sunrise and hoping to see if theres benefit to my digestion.

When im fasted i feel my best, as im sure we all do. However if i could fast forever I would so i dont get ‘sick’

### Sean Waters — 2018-11-23T15:59:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-fog-cure.22521/post-256321>

I think 2 lots of 30 mins is meant to be better than 1 hour in CT anyway mate.

A lot of the new members are told to focus on the big things, Blue Light/EMF avoidance, more Sun and CT.

But if I were you - and what I am now doing after 2 years on this blog - is keep doing those things - but start reading Jack's blogs from the start:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/all-posts-pdf-in-one-zip-file-for-download.18666/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/all-posts-pdf-in-one-zip-file-for-download.18666/)

All Jack's blogs in chronological order. I print off 10 at a time and just read and study and digest.

Also, I really like the Leptin Reset diet man, think it's somewhere around blog #40. I think way too many newbies overlook that diet because Jack doesn't talk about it anymore/ says food doesn't matter. As more senior members, we have to get you to focus on EMF and CT etc. which will help you faster and without having to explain everything in one comment (which is impossible). You'll learn if you stick around, this is a process - not a one comment solution.

But that Leptin Reset IMO is really really important to get your Gut and Peripheral Clocks RESET and in tune with your Brain Clock. For me, all the other hacks like CT and Sun etc. didn't work as well before I did it... even though I was Keto and in very good shape (I assumed I didn't need it).

I've noticed tremendous changes from 3 months on that diet. I believe it is still really relevant and effective. It will also get you looking at the foundations of Jack's work and tie in with the first 100 blogs, including the Cold series and the Brain Gut/ Seafood Evolution of Humans blogs... it's a nice place to build your knowledge from.

I'm currently at blog 150 of 300. EMF #3 I think....

# Thread: Hello from New Park, PA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-park-pa.22522/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jillhershey73 — 2018-11-18T04:55:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-park-pa.22522/post-255873>

Hello my name is Jill. I am 45 years old, a single mom and an overworked and underpaid school teacher. I have struggled with my weight my entire life and have also had periods of time where I have suffered from extreme fatigue. Hypothyroidism runs in my entire family, including my brothers, aunts, cousins and mother. I suffer from it as well. In addition to hypothyroidism, diabetes runs in my family We do not have any Type 1 but both my parents are on the verge of Type 2. I have been eating a high fat, low carb and protein diet. Even with this type of diet and Glucophage my 1 and 2 hr PP's and fasting morning glucose are higher than they should be. Not crazy high but anywhere between 90 and 110 with the occasional 85ish. I have also stayed away from exercise because I'm pretty sure my hormones are not ready for it.

I know in order to be optimal I know I need to reset my hormones and my circadian rhythms in order to lower my inflammation and improve my body composition. Right now I'm about 36% body fat which is way too high. I had had it tested through the BodPod. Anyway, I have a pretty open minded doctor who is open to prescribing me Cytomel and Glucophage (Brand name Metformin) as well as letting me try other new things. I am currently taking both although I am not sure I am taking them correctly so that they benefit me in addition to beginning Dr. Jack's protocols.

Since I'm a new member I have started at the beginning with the webinars trying to figure out a logical sequence for all these protocols. Going to run a set of labs to get baseline of lots of things. I am excited to get them all started but overwhelmed with all the information! I have a few questions that some of you might be able to answer to help me make some decisions:

1\. What blue light blocking glasses, computer/phone screen filters and light bulbs do you recommend? I have looked at the products from the company LowBlueLights. I have already installed Iris software on both my laptop and my son's Chromebook to help this.

2\. Do the clear phone/computer filters and glasses work? Do they need to have the yellow tint to be effective? I have also looked at the Ocushield company which sells blue light blocking products but they are clear.

3\. I need to buy a new mattress. I know not to buy ones with metal coils. I have always liked Temperpedic but seeing a lot of recommendations for latex. Any suggestions? What mattresses work with the Magnetico sleep pad I see mentioned?

4\. How do you recommend taking Glucophage (brand name Metformin) and Cytomel? Glucophage an hour before food? Should I take T3 based on my circadian rhythms like Paul Robinson suggests? I am on about 30 mcgs Cytomel and 2000 mg instant release Glucohpage. I am hoping that these protocols will eventually correct my imbalances so that I no longer need these meds.

5\. I heard on a webinar that you can also use a low .8 mcg dosage of Cycloset if your cortisol is upside down after saliva testing. Can you take both Cycloset and Glucophage? Just curious at this point.

6\. For CT, does the ice vest work as well as a cold bathtub of water? And how often for a woman?

Thank you all! Looking forward to learning together!

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-18T06:02:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-park-pa.22522/post-255875>

1\. What blue light blocking glasses, computer/phone screen filters and light bulbs do you recommend? I have looked at the products from the company LowBlueLights. I have already installed Iris software on both my laptop and my son's Chromebook to help this.

**[www.RaOptics.io](http://www.RaOptics.io) is what I use. Member code is "Optimal"**

2\. Do the clear phone/computer filters and glasses work? Do they need to have the yellow tint to be effective? I have also looked at the Ocushield company which sells blue light blocking products but they are clear.

**The film must block blue. You can use a spectroscope to confirm blue blockade 400-550nm.**

3\. I need to buy a new mattress. I know not to buy ones with metal coils. I have always liked Temperpedic but seeing a lot of recommendations for latex. Any suggestions? What mattresses work with the Magnetico sleep pad I see mentioned?

**Buy one without metal springs.**

4\. How do you recommend taking Glucophage (brand name Metformin) and Cytomel? Glucophage an hour before food? Should I take T3 based on my circadian rhythms like Paul Robinson suggests? I am on about 30 mcgs Cytomel and 2000 mg instant release Glucophage. I am hoping that these protocols will eventually correct my imbalances so that I no longer need these meds.

**I usually don't recommend clinical decisions on the website. T3 and T4 are made via AM sunlight exposure of our skin and eye. I use that before I jump to drugs**

5\. I heard on a webinar that you can also use a low .8 mcg dosage of Cycloset if your cortisol is upside down after saliva testing. Can you take both Cycloset and Glucophage? Just curious at this point.

**That should be discussed with your PCP but it can be done in many patients.**

6\. For CT, does the ice vest work as well as a cold bathtub of water? And how often for a woman?

**CT is always a benefit so any amount is good.**

### jillhershey73 — 2018-11-18T15:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-new-park-pa.22522/post-255891>

Thank you so much! I’ll take this all into consideration! I have a lot to learn!

# Thread: Tests I should Get before starting my Gold membership?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tests-i-should-get-before-starting-my-gold-membership.22523/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ALEXIS TUDOR — 2018-11-18T20:21:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tests-i-should-get-before-starting-my-gold-membership.22523/post-255913>

I am realizing at this point in my research(after about a month) it would be good to get some direct advice from Dr. Kruse so I was thinking of starting with the Gold membership soon. I wanted to have some tests on hand because I have not had any recently and in the last 18 months have shifted out from being mostly vegan to a gradual meat eater. In the last 3 weeks trying to follow something close to the leptin Rx prolly about 85% of the time which will hopefully grow towards a 100% as my body adapts to the new diet. Other than getting a standard blood test, but do mitochondriacs commonly do specific non-standard ones? I realize the n=1 phenomena here, but there there are some universal tests most folk do.

Some things I need to consider: I am on a school bus drivers income so while I can spend money on some things/test I really try and get value for my buck. Naturally if I can get incremental health improvements I can generate more income. I have health issues that revolve around being underweight with low stamina, MS like issues at times but never any official diagnosis. Stamina is not so low that i cannot work at all but definitely part time. My only other concern is, for the next 2-3 months I will also unfortunately be in a friend's house where I will not be able to control NNemf's and lighting issues other than my room so it will be a compromise there other than staying in my room at night in low light which I have been doing as much as possible. After that I will move into my own place a bit out of town.

Thanks in advance for the advice.

# Thread: BPA free = a marketing scheme parents.......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa-free-a-marketing-scheme-parents.22524/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-19T01:09:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa-free-a-marketing-scheme-parents.22524/post-255927>

BPA free = a marketing scam. It is replaced by another chemical that causes more issues.

It’s a marketing scam, folks. BPA free does NOT = SAFE to use.

“University of Calgary scientists say they think their research is the first to show that bisphenol-S, an ingredient in many products bearing “BPA-free” labels, causes abnormal growth surges of neurons in an animal embryo.”

“A lot of the alternative chemicals have not been adequately tested because they don’t have to be,” said lead author Deborah Kurrasch. “A compound is considered safe (by the Food and Drug Administration) until proven otherwise.”
[https://www.washingtonpost.com/nati...ecc37e-9a7e-11e4-a7ee-526210d665b4\_story.html](https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/bpa-alternative-disrupts-normal-brain-cell-growth-is-tied-to-hyperactivity-study-says/2015/01/12/a9ecc37e-9a7e-11e4-a7ee-526210d665b4_story.html)

### JanSz — 2018-11-19T15:08:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa-free-a-marketing-scheme-parents.22524/post-255944>

> [Jason Yun said:](/goto/post?id=255932)
>
> So do you recommend glass? Stainless steel? Or what?

Stainless Steel=iron, carbon, chromium, nickel
It is crystalline structure.

In presence CL, chlorine and time that crystalline structure changes, structures made of it degrades. stability
That factor is important enough in Nuclear Power Plants that for Class 1 piping their chlorine exposure must be limited.

............
Not sure if that makes a difference in drinking utensils.
Not sure if switches in crystalline structures may cause some nickel release.

.................

# Thread: Pharmasan labs?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pharmasan-labs.22529/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### James Gerard — 2018-11-19T22:29:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pharmasan-labs.22529/post-255996>

So Today I have been collecting and ruminating on how I want to approach doctors in getting labs. One of the labs I was looking at from the Heal your hormones bootcamp is the Stimulated Cytokine lab from Pharmasan labs. I am very late to the game because Pharmasan labs IS CLOSED! as it says on their website. But I managed to find a partially illuminating article on it that is approximately a year old that I thought all mitochondriacs should take a look at.

[https://chartingdoctorterritory.wordpress.com/2017/11/03/why-pharmasan-labs-is-on-my-sht-list/](https://chartingdoctorterritory.wordpress.com/2017/11/03/why-pharmasan-labs-is-on-my-sht-list/)

For the sake of prosperity I will copy and paste it below.

**Why Pharmasan Labs is on my sh\*t list**

An increasing number of people are developing autoimmune disease. And an increasing number of people are googling their symptoms. Invariably, they’ll come across what’s known as the “Th1/Th2” system. This system itself asks a question: which one are you–Th1 or Th2?

It’s a simplistic way of looking at it, because few people are strictly Th1 or Th2. Yet, it remains among the public (and uninformed doctors) a multiple-choice question without an option “C” in the answer bank. You’re treated as having one or the other.

Members of the general public (and those same doctors I just mentioned) have a tendency to get stuck on keywords and buzzphrases, latching onto a trendy concept and beating it to death. (MTHFR, anyone?) Among autoimmune communities, the Th1/Th2 topic is what the MTHFR obsession is to the detoxification crowd.

Several supplement companies arose to the demand, developing a Th1-stimulating formula, and a Th2-stimulating formula. They packaged samples of each in little bags, complete with instructions for a “6-day clinical challenge”. Patients would take the first formula for the first 3 days, and the second formula for Days 4-6. They were advised to report back to their doctor any symptoms they felt, and in response to which formula.

Most people think the Th1/Th2 question is a new thing. It’s not. It had been the accepted model since the 1980s, when we only knew about those two players.

Things have changed, kids. Enter Th17, the “new” kid on the block, although investigative research didn’t really take hold in earnest until about 2006. Yes, that’s right – that far back. (Most people didn’t know about it until around 2012, and even now, in 2017, it gets glossed over.) The “new” Th17 player did answer a few conundrums that kept popping up like a carnival whack-a-mole game: why people undergoing the Th1/Th2 “clinical challenge” were reacting badly to both the Th1- and the Th2-stimulating supplement formulas.

It’s bad enough to feel symptoms in response to one formula, but both??

Let’s not ignore the elephant in the room: symptoms are the body’s way of saying “this sucks! I’m getting damaged here!”

The “clinical challenges” were flaring people’s autoimmune symptoms up, for days, weeks, or even months.

“First do no harm” indeed.

Enter a lab to the rescue!

Word on the clinical hill began to spread about a lab test panel that would test blood samples for immune reactivity instead. It was only as invasive as a blood draw, and once at the lab, the lab techs could hit the sample with all several types of immune stimulants and measure the response of different immunological biomarkers.

Better yet, that panel was connected up with saliva and urine samples to get an idea of what was also going on in the nervous and endocrine systems, since they have a significant impact on immune function. So do lectins and bacterial endotoxins, which were _also_ included in this panel!

Lab testing FTW! Pharmasan Labs became my new hero, the Stimulated Cytokine Test my holy grail.

Only when this lab “super-panel” was recommended to me by an experienced an unbiased colleague I have come to deeply respect did I actually start recommending it to my patients.

One drawback: it was god-awful expensive. But, this lab was the only one doing this type of test, and we (the doctors in the trenches) needed this crucial information.

All was calm and all was bright until one day, probably about 2 years ago: we received a notice, either by email or snail mail, that the lab would be discontinuing certain test codes and changing the available analytes on the existing tests.

My beloved lectin sensitivity results (the PHA biomarkers) were going away, in favor of the more-generic “candida” stimulant. In addition, the report format would be changing, and not in a good way. The previous way was relatively readable:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

And:

(The blurriness notwithstanding. Thanks, Russian web pirates.)

As opposed to the new format:

(Don’t worry, I’ll take an anonymized picture and insert it here. I couldn’t find a sample report online, not even on the lab’s website. Not exactly shocking.)

We contacted Pharmasan Labs and flat-out told them that these new changes were unwise and unnecessary. We had no idea what to make of the new report format, as there didn’t seem to be any consistency between where the actual numbers fell compared to the given reference range, and what was graphically depicted as “high” or “low”.

Come to think of it, that had been a bit of a problem before. And I also noticed the same problem with their urinary transmitter testing.

But to give the PHA/lectin marker the axe, that was a dealbreaker. Continuing with my flat-out-ness, I stated that the lectin sensitivity section was one of the primary reasons for my running this lab panel, and the lab would not do well to remove one of its biggest draws, one of the chief selling points that separated them from every other lab out there.

Because if need be, I could take every other component of that “super-panel” and piece it together from other labs. The adrenal component could be obtained (easily and cheaply) from another lab, as could all hormones, and even some urinary neurotransmitters, if I felt like it.

Beyond the stimulation of the immune system, Pharmasan didn’t really have anything I was interested in that I couldn’t get somewhere else.

And they wanted to kill it.

Bad move.

I must not have been the only doctor who raised a stink, because for a while, they listened. But like so many other corporate and government initiatives in contemporary society, they said, “Okay, okay, we’ll behave ourselves, give you what you want, and bring back what we were doing before. We won’t change, it’s okay!”, but then later, quietly, when no one’s looking, they’ll hit the Retry button on their initiative and hope nobody notices–or that at least it’s accepted this time.

I guess not a whole lot of doctors expressed their discontent this time around, because the changes went through, and they stuck.

Maybe the changes stuck because the lab itself wasn’t as forthcoming this time, or perhaps they were, but not as far in advance.

Proof that they offered this test before (from their (outdated) [CPT codes page](https://www.pharmasan.com/index.php?p=cms&cid=558&pid=557)):

Proof that they **don’t** now (from their test menu):

Yeah, it’s gone. You can’t order it.

They were on their way out of my office before, but then came the kicker: they were [sanctioned in court](https://www.justice.gov/usao-wdwi/pr/osceola-laboratory-agrees-pay-85-million-resolve-false-billing-case), something relating to fraudulent Medicare billing, occurring over the course of years, complete with an $8.5 million dollar tag.

Whoops.

Once I started digging, I found that there have also been some [CLIA issues](https://www.cms.gov/Regulations-and-Guidance/Legislation/CLIA/Downloads/regis2014.pdf) (PDF). Although I believe they’ve regained their CLIA certification since then, I had heard through the grapevine that it was in danger of being revoked again, this time for issues with consistency, after a doctor mailed in 2 test kits containing material taken from the same sample and received wildly different results, but I can’t find any instance of that having made the public record, so I can’t verify that. The juicy tidbit came from a very trusted source, though, who has no vendetta or other reason to falsify something like that.

Obviously, I’m looking into other options. Pharmasan Labs is no longer in my office.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-19T22:34:29+0200

# Thread: 23 male libido and CT. Thank you Jack!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23-male-libido-and-ct-thank-you-jack.22530/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MattD1995 — 2018-11-19T22:34:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23-male-libido-and-ct-thank-you-jack.22530/post-256002>

I’m a 23 year old male. I was a healthy, fit guy (or so I thought) until I was struck with severe chronic fatigue, hypothyroidism, and insomnia at age 19. It ended my college football career, destroyed my social/dating life, forced me to drop out of school, and left me almost completely incapacitated for the last 4 years aside from my full-time job.

I’ve spent the last 4 years battling my ever increasing myriad of symptoms. I’ve seen multiple doctors (both alternative and mainstream). I’ve spent around 30K on diets, supplements, hormone therapies, etc... that did little to nothing.

Since finding this website and the wealth of information Dr Kruse has provided, my health has taken a noticeable uptick for the better. In just 2 months, a simple daily routine of sunlight, sleeping grounded in my back yard, blue blockers, and oysters + salmon have done more for me than the last 4 years of treatments have.

Despite all my improvements, my libido continued to remain in the tank until 2 weeks ago. In the last 14 days, I’ve begun incorporating EZ water + daily CT in my pool. The results have been nothing short of astounding. After 4 years of being basically asexual, my libido has come roaring back and has given me a massive boost of hope and confidence.

I still have a long way to go and a lot of adjustments to make to my environment, but for the first time in years I have hope. It feels so good to be doing something that’s actually WORKING after wasting 4 years and thousands of dollars on treatments and doctors who did NOTHING for me. It feels so good to begin to understand my body and it’s relation to my environment through the lens of Dr Kruse’s extensive work.

Just wanted to share my mini-success story. I’m sure I’ll have another in a year’s time as I continue to progress and take responsibility for my body and my environment. The mitochondriac life isn’t an easy one, but it’s worth it. Thank you Dr Kruse!

> /

Absolutely! After seeing them mentioned by Dr Kruse in several posts, I drink Gerolsteiner and Mountain Valley spring water almost exclusively now. Both come from springs and are high in natural mineral content. My understanding from Dr Gerald Pollack’s work is that EZ/structured water is formed in nature via the movement of water over rocks. I’ve heard him and many others mention a vortex machine that can be used to structure one’s own water, but I havent been able to afford one yet.

For the first time in my life, I actually enjoy water. Gerolsteiner especially really feels normal to me. I could never drink enough water growing up. It was mostly fluoridated and made me feel sick and dehydrated.

### Pablo — 2018-11-22T14:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23-male-libido-and-ct-thank-you-jack.22530/post-256271>

I am very happy of your improvements Matt!! Just out of curiosity, where did your problems come from to begin with? I am also feeling better with the CT. You are way ahead of me with the sleeping grounded and the EZ water... I´ll have to level up in the next months.. Take care

### MattD1995 — 2018-11-23T03:13:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23-male-libido-and-ct-thank-you-jack.22530/post-256301>

Thank you! My problems started around 4 years ago after a series of football related surgeries. Looking back, I missed a lot of warning signs my body had been giving me in highschool. I was already on a physical decline prior to the surgeries, the surgeries just brought it to the surface.

In the last few months, I’ve come to believe the root cause was my wisdom teeth surgery back in my junior year of highschool. The original dentist left dead bone and pockets of debris in my jaw that lead to cavitations.

I had ozone injections in all four quadrants just 3 days ago, and I’m already noticing significant improvement in my symptoms.

>
> Since finding this website and the wealth of information Dr Kruse has provided, my health has taken a noticeable uptick for the better. In just 2 months, a simple daily routine of sunlight, sleeping grounded in my back yard, blue blockers, and oysters + salmon have done more for me than the last 4 years of treatments have.
>
> Despite all my improvements, my libido continued to remain in the tank until 2 weeks ago. In the last 14 days, I’ve begun incorporating EZ water + daily CT in my pool. The results have been nothing short of astounding. After 4 years of being basically asexual, my libido has come roaring back and has given me a massive boost of hope and confidence.
>
> I still have a long way to go and a lot of adjustments to make to my environment, but for the first time in years I have hope. It feels so good to be doing something that’s actually WORKING after wasting 4 years and thousands of dollars on treatments and doctors who did NOTHING for me. >
> Just wanted to share my mini-success story. I’m sure I’ll have another in a year’s time as I continue to progress and take responsibility for my body and my environment. The mitochondriac life isn’t an easy one, but it’s worth it. Thank you Dr Kruse!

# Thread: Cel Phones with Less Radiation?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cel-phones-with-less-radiation.22533/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GringoPerdido — 2018-11-20T17:17:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cel-phones-with-less-radiation.22533/post-256075>

Are there cel phones and laptops that give off less radiation?

### Inger — 2018-11-20T17:45:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cel-phones-with-less-radiation.22533/post-256079>

well, I use a old, Not Smart, Samsung and Nokia, no internet or bluetooth etc. Just very simple.
Wen not in use my meter shows nothing on them. When I call or mesage of course it is horrible. But I rarely use it tho.

For computer you need to hardwire it and switch off any wifi or bluetooth etc and it should not have any much radiation at all. At least that is what I do. Meter shows really good readings. I also use corded extra keyboard and mouse to be further away from it. All is corded.

### Peter Schmidt — 2019-02-01T07:25:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cel-phones-with-less-radiation.22533/post-262014>

Hi Robin
I'm using a vest case (when I need to hold it, it folds the shielding under) and a defender shield case that clips to my belt - [www.defendershield.com](http://www.defendershield.com)
On my iPhone 6 I've turned down the radiation by going into Mobile / Mobile Data Options/ and Turned off 4G so it reverts back to 3G - in case I need to use Data it has cut down the RF to the Yellow range on my meter - it may be different outside Australia. I monitor the settings a few times a day to make sure Data, Wifi and Bluetooth are turned off. If I dont need to be "on call" (I'm an IT Manager) but I need to carry it, I turn aeroplane mode on and check once every few hours (turn it off) if anyone has rung or left a message. It turn it to aeroplane mode when in the car, lift as the signal goes much higher to reach the nearest towers....
I hope that helps.

Thanks
Peter in Oz.

# Thread: Sun was the missing link
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-was-the-missing-link.22534/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scott Hardin — 2018-11-20T19:41:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-was-the-missing-link.22534/post-256095>

My neighbors in Atlanta think I’m crazy when I’m tanning at the pool in 45 degree weather on my lunch break. I never would have believed that 1) I wouldn’t feel cold in the slightest and 2) AM and afternoon sun exposure would cure my depression and even move my mood and outlook into the “annoyingly positive” direction.

I have a suntouch plus and a red light therapy box for the rainy, cloudy, barely above freezing days. I’ve also added black lighting to my home to supplement the lack of UV through the window, but nothing is the same as actual sunlight on my whole body.

BTW - If the forecast is sunny, I also wear tan through swim trunks as boxer shorts so I can strip down and get as close to naked as possible when the opportunity arises.

Ultimately though,the difference is so dramatic I think I’m going to move to a lower latitude.

### drezy — 2018-11-20T21:59:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-was-the-missing-link.22534/post-256109>

Great Scott!

I'm happy for you and your findings.

Who knew that minor changes considered bizarre by most people carry so much benefit.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-21T01:03:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-was-the-missing-link.22534/post-256145>

Small changes that lead to transformation = non-linear stimulus

### JackieNewZealand — 2019-01-23T03:36:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sun-was-the-missing-link.22534/post-261081>

Thanks for posting. I've been finding the same results. Sun exposure over as much skin as possible, except I cover facial skin as don't want to look 90 too soon! Spent all morning at the beach in NZealand and my mood is off the charts positive. The winters here are cold and dark with short sunlight, so that will be the real test in another 6 months. Tell others of your findings because depression is such a horrible condition and so many more could be helped. Best to you.

### Sheddie — 2018-11-27T03:21:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-holiday-feasts.22536/post-256668>

> [Christine\_L said:](/goto/post?id=256619)
>
> [@Sheddie](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20386/) any other liver recipes?

About the only way I can enjoy liver is in a very blender-whipped or food-processor pate. This recipe looks about usual -- [https://www.furtherfood.com/recipe/...ida-leaky-gut-food-prebiotic-gut-health-diet/](https://www.furtherfood.com/recipe/grass-fed-beef-liver-pate-recipe-candida-leaky-gut-food-prebiotic-gut-health-diet/)
although I recall brandy and garlic, even butter, in ones I made. It's especially nice if really smooth and close to a mousse consistency, all chalky-gritty texture gone on taste test. Push it through a sieve if the on hand equipment doesn't quite get it smooth enough.

It will get more firm once chilled so factor that in, so raw veggies or paleo crackers can dip in easily on serving... Bacon might add a salty kick. I remember liver pate leftovers went well with breakfast eggs... These days, I confess I'm more on the lookout for pork liver pate products at the farmers' market than feeling up to DIY!!

# Thread: Question regarding FB page referenced in All Things Seafood for the Skeptic
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glenn A — 2018-11-21T01:36:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256152>

This is my first post on the forum. Since I haven't posted before, and also haven't read more than a couple dozen posts here, I did some searching to see if my question might already be entered here. I can't find the information I'm looking for, either here or in the FB page where Jack begins "Eat your damn sea food...":

[https://www.facebook.com/drjackkrus...otif\_id=1535324462237544&notif\_t=feed\_comment](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/2292376074160067?comment_id=2292950894102585&reply_comment_id=2292952037435804&notif_id=1535324462237544&notif_t=feed_comment)

I've listened to hours of Jack's YouTube lectures. I definitely know he pushes us to "get DHA". From everything I've read or heard from him, and from his followers I infer he isn't an advocate of letting our bodies derive DHA from alpha-linolinic acid (ALA). Or doesn't allow for that amount of DHA to be sufficient.

This immediately brings on skepticism. Both from my research, and from my personal experience. I'm 76 years old and my health and eyes work beautifully. I haven't taken a supplement that contained any omega-3 of any kind for at least 5 years. I look at my computer screen way too much, always with blue blocking, and the same for TV. I'm outside a lot, but not enough. But i do get early morning sunshine while grounded, and also run sprints grounded, regardless of the weather or season. I hardly eat a vegetable that hasn't been picked from my own garden (thus inseason) within 15 minutes of ingestion, and almost all my meat, eggs and cheese is either organic or grass fed.

Since I don't consume any sources of compromised omega-6, or even excess omega-6, I'm just guessing that I'm getting enough ALA that isn't competing with omega-6 for resources, such that my body provides all the DHA I need to keep my eyes, brain, etc. healthy and regenerating properly every single day.

So I would love to see a study that proves that I have this all wrong. I forgot to say that I rarely eat fish. Why is it not possible that a paleo diet without a trace of fish or DHA supplements may provide a human body with sufficient ALA to yield a very healthy level of DHA?

### Jack Kruse — 2018-11-21T02:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256156>

A cursory review of the human literature would show you humans do not convert ALA well at all. 0.9% in men 1-3% in women.

Your Bazan effect in your eye and liver is likely your benefit. Everyone has a N=1.

Continue to be a skeptic. It serves a deep purpose.

### Glenn A — 2018-11-21T19:23:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256215>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=256156)
>
> A cursory review of the human literature would show you humans do not convert ALA well at all. 0.9% in men 1-3% in women.
>
> Your Bazan effect in your eye and liver is likely your benefit. Everyone has a N=1.
>
> Continue to be a skeptic. It serves a deep purpose.

Thanks, especially for mention of Bazan. I reviewed your article on the Bazan effect,

[https://jackkruse.com/reality-4-eye-brain-diseases-exploding/](https://jackkruse.com/reality-4-eye-brain-diseases-exploding/)

a very valuable piece of the puzzle to me, as I was unfamiliar with much of the information there. To summarize, for this discussion though, I found evidence that the human body has great abilities to recycle DHA. So not to diminish the facts expained in your writing, it does seem to indicate that our bodies may maintain levels of DHA in spite some carelessness on our part to provide new DHA input.

This is logically proven by man's history. While it's hard to show that in prehistoric times man didn't eat and also feed his children plenty of animal brains, it's fairly obvious that modern man doesn't eat brains much, nor fish, yet human offspring have a tremendous ability to create DHA enough for a very healthy brain and a set of eyes, all from virtual "junk food", and mostly _in utero_ plus during the first 2 years after birth. It seems that the brain DHA is then maintained also with minimal cell replacement, almost without attention to diet. I'm not saying this is acceptable nutrition, just that something is happening to hold DHA levels at a fairly acceptable level in brain and eye tissue. Your Bazan article explains some of this.

When I first began delving into the advisability of continuing to take daily doses of omega-3 supplements, I read on the subject for over a year. I encountered the writings of Brian Peskin, who at the time (2010-2011) worked as chief research scientist at Cambridge International Institute for Medical Science, but had previously been in the Pharmacy departments at Texas Southern University, and at the Houston Academy of Medicine. I don't actively follow Peskin now as he has gone on to sell his own brand of parent omega-6/omega-3 supplement and may no longer write totally in the interest of science. However, from his website 10 years ago he made available many papers that seemed to show that he found studies to prove that what the human body derives from both LA and ALA is exactly the amount of the derivatives that the body needs at the moment, including AA, DHA, and also the eicosanoids that derive from the first level derivatives. So talking about low percentages of DHA that can result from breaking down parent products and mentioning "poor ability to convert" as a characteristic of the human body can really, per Peskin, be thought of more as merely common phrases used by the fish oil industry, which as we know, is in the business of selling omega-3. Never does the industry mention that the best way to effectively use the omega-3 that you consume is to first restrict all consumption of compromised or degraded omega-6 oils.

From my studies, after finding this "inefficient conversion" jargon in most fish-oil sales pieces, and in many alternative health writers who seem to just cut and paste the fish-oil arguments into their own articles, i seriously distrust the need for supplementation with something as drastic as (what Peskin calls) _pharmacological overdoses_ of DHA and EPA via fish or krill oil.

Peskin's website no longer has as many of his lecture videos or papers as it used to, probably due to his move to marketing a supplement instead of just his lectures and books. For this reason, I will provide here just an example of Peskins ideas, including a brief introduction to his past work so that you might see that even in 2011, he was talking about "quantum nutrition". From this little article though, you might realize that Peskin already at that time wasn't in favor of doing just a "cursory review of human literature" on DHA derivation and maintenance/recycling in human physiology....

[http://brianpeskin.com/pdf/publications/2011-March-Smart-Life-Real-Science-PEOs.pdf](http://brianpeskin.com/pdf/publications/2011-March-Smart-Life-Real-Science-PEOs.pdf)

so following his lead, I remain a skeptic.

The reason I bring up this question on the forum is to strive for some truth as to what happens, and to explore the possibility that, as you yourself and many others know, some damage may be done when excess amounts of any substance is introduced into our bodies. From reading many of the questions here on the forum, I sense a blind striving for "more" and "what quality" and "what source" with respect to DHA, and while I don't want the questions to go unanswered, I do feel it's important to answer in a way that lets people realize that many/most of us _might be_ obtaining/maintaining optimum levels of DHA just by eating a high percentage of very fine, organic foods, while, of course, also getting good balances of light, rest, exercise, and bliss in our days.

I'm happy to have discovered this forum, and of course welcome all input.

I may post a bit later some clinical evidence that omega-3 supplementation, if excessive, can cause serious psychological issues. Readers here at least need to realize that for omega-3 as for most substances, more is not better.

### Glenn A — 2018-11-21T22:39:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256240>

Patricia Kane has a practice which includes measuring and correcting omega-3 lipids. I happened on some of her work years ago, but the page I wished to cite is no longer available as they have changed their approach and their website. However, here is a similar page that still has 3 thumbnail sketches of patients they have treated who were suffering from an overdose of omega-3 supplements. Removal of the omega-3 supplements fixes the condition. Here is what Kane's "BodyBio" web site still has on this issue:

[https://www.bodybio.com/blog/fat-facts-on-omega-3-and-omega-6-fatty-acids/](https://www.bodybio.com/blog/fat-facts-on-omega-3-and-omega-6-fatty-acids/)

Despite the fact that BodyBio now sells a supplement, it seems they have properly represented the value of omega-3 lipids and not overdone the importance of getting sufficient ALA, DHA and EPA in the diet. I've always learned a lot about PUFA from Patricia Kane's writings.

In case one thinks that someone selling supplements cannot take on a scientific point of view, here is a paper by Patricia Kane published 19 years ago in _The Journal of Orthomolecular Medicine_ far before she had a supplement formulated:
_
[http://orthomolecular.org/library/jom/1999/articles/1999-v14n02-p103.shtml](http://orthomolecular.org/library/jom/1999/articles/1999-v14n02-p103.shtml)
_
I notice there is an old discussion on the forum titled "How much DHA is too much". Perhaps some of us might provide an up to date answer to that question after a bit more study. The question was posed by Aerose91 in 2014. Does it seem properly answered at this time?

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-much-dha-is-too-much.11026/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-much-dha-is-too-much.11026/)

There are a lot of interesting, and not necessarily conflicting answers posted there.....

### Linz — 2018-11-22T15:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256273>

Hello Glenn,
Just a few of my own thoughts here, and an observation that. as a woman, I am quite certain that I donated rather a lot of my own DHA to my offspring rather than leaving them to create their own!

You are very fortunate to be so healthy. I too had few health problems until I began using a brightly lit screen to do admin work at night after my children had gone to bed. Not long afterwards all hell was let loose in my immune system (more or less back on track thanks to Dr K). Some 40 years ago I was taught that DHA was well conserved and also, but not discussed here very often, that although regular oily fish was essential in the diet for DHA, too much was toxic because it unbalanced vits A and D. Now our vitamin A and D processing are both being assaulted by artificial light and nnEMFs and DHA is broken down to resolvins, protectins and maresins as protective mechanism. Have you considered how these changes in the environment can invalidate earlier research and increase requirement for DHA? Even Icelandic ponies (herbivores) are now being given some herring to help them through the winter. Also, much of the research into PUFAs was done using isolated, processed oils and neither seasonal changes nor quantum behaviour were acounted for. Have you read any of Prof Michael Crawford's research on DHA?
Another aspect is the requirement for DHA in a low light arctic environment with uncoupled mito haplotype v the requirement in the tropics with closely coupled mito, not to mention all those striving to find a balance while living in the wrong environment.

I have Patricia's book. I do not see it being sold now.
There is a number of cases there.
One can conclude from looking at them that the neolithic diseases are messing up our fatty acids with result of highly excessive omega3 (contains DHA), or possibly how the is how body is attempting defense.
Over years here, I posted questions about it and were given answers, you may want to look them up.
Over years here, I looked over numerous fatty acids analysis of members here, there are in my posts.
There is a O6/O3 ratio posted 4:1 without much explanation of what it means.
Most people interpret that as something that one should have when testing.
At Patricia Kane old site I got that it is about desirable proportion of LA/ALA in food one eat.

..
Keep this interesting topic alive. Highly interesting.

..
As far as I am aware, Patricia's research ended on peroxisome that do not do their job properly and that allows very long fatty acids to accumulate.

> [View attachment 9747](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/9747/)

Very interesting, JanSz! I will start searching your posts here on omega-3. I used to study on this daily and have hundreds of links to articles I keep on hand. When I followed Brian Peskin closely, at that time he was reading 400-600 studies per week, looking for good ones, which turned out to be less than 3% of what is published. So it's very handy to have others do some "weeding" for us! I appreciate that you've been researching this.

Yes, Kane and her husband also got heavily into this, and shared a lot of the same thinking as Peskin. I believe the Kane's got their 4:1 ratio from the studies done by Shlomo Yehuda who determined the optimum ratio of omega-6 to omega-3 (in rats) in the brain as 4:1. I see in the Kane article's references that Yehuda's work is cited. As Peskin points out, different organs have vastly different ratio's of 6:3. Our bodies determine this per the need of the different organs, and Jack Kruse confirms this in his findings, so personally, I don't worry about the ratio I take in, as long as I avoid like the plague any excess of omega-6 that would be coming from refined seed oils, as in fried and junk foods, as these already oxidized omega's are very damaging when installed in our membranes. I'm guessing what one gets from whole natural foods though, in ratio's from 1:1 up to 6:1 as you find in free-range domestic cattle muscle meat is just fine. BTW, spinach has way more omega-3 than omega-6, I believe about a 1:4 ratio. The only vegetable I know, outside of some seeds, that is like this.

I will start looking at your earlier posts, but here's the study on the 4:1 ratio superiority of Yehuda when looking at brain performance only:

[http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.328.3085&rep=rep1&type=pdf](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.328.3085&rep=rep1&type=pdf)

What stuck in my head is that:
DHA have a very long half life (good) (little goes long way)
EPA ------>seafood have a very high content of it
and it is hard to lower ALA and EPA when they become high (so also long half life)
Oysters are the only seafood that I know of that have DHA and low EPA (and is Jacks favorite food)

and saw those analysis used in reference to (normal) (expensive) oysters
Jack makes a big deal about DHA being on sn-2 position (that supposedly is questionable when using supplements
\---
I do not get that part because:
DHA is a separate piece
position, there are three of them
separate DHA goes where it wants to go

triglycerides first have to break into pieces, then they go where they want or are being pushed to go
\---
DHA is made initially in algae (that is not in seafood)

always hand waving,

Have you considered how these changes in the environment can invalidate earlier research and increase requirement for DHA? Even Icelandic ponies (herbivores) are now being given some herring to help them through the winter. Also, much of the research into PUFAs was done using isolated, processed oils and neither seasonal changes nor quantum behaviour were acounted for. Have you read any of Prof Michael Crawford's research on DHA?
> Another aspect is the requirement for DHA in a low light arctic environment with uncoupled mito haplotype v the requirement in the tropics with closely coupled mito, not to mention all those striving to find a balance while living in the wrong environment.

Some really valuable thoughts here, and thanks Linz! Yes, I don't remember exact percentages, but a lot of the infant's brain, and central nervous system DNA is deposited before birth. It had to come from the food you ate! I doubt it can result from your body depleting what you already have built into you.

I appreciate that you seem to have learned a lot due to Dr K's influence. I'm just starting to build up my knowledge base from his work and have a long way to go to catch up with you. I haven't read yet that high DHA levels unbalance vitamins A & D. Can you provide a reference on that?

On the mention that unnatural environments with nnEMFs can raise the need for higher intake of vitamins and DHA, yes that makes sense. Only recently have I begun some of Dr K's recommendation on controlling the visual spectrum to which I expose my eyes, 24 hours a day. But like you, for years I've been gazing into computer screens, with eyes unprotected. Now I block the blue, and increase the IR at other times, especially in the early morning when I can get it directly from the sun without much UV. I am 76 years old, and realize I may have a body more vulnerable to all kinds of threats than people half my age. So when I find new data that seems reasonable, I tend to jump right in a try to learn more. That's why I am here, and glad I found this forum.

On our increased need for DHA, I agree. I just feel that I live so differently, so primitively, yet wisely, compared to most of modern mankind, that I am probably maintaining a higher level of omega-3 availability than almost any in the human race have had available in the past, (and consistently, day after day). I don't eat fish that may have preformed DHA. Nor do I eat brains that may have loads of DHA. But I obviously have a built in ability to derive DHA from ALA or I would be showing signs, as you say, of deterioration. So my assumption is, I'm getting enough ALA, and my body is giving me the DHA I need. There are studies that show that there is some variability in how much DHA can be derived from the parent omega-3. In no way can an adult vary their ability to turn ALA into DHA such that they can match a 1 year old infant's conversion rate, but it's proven that vegetarians and vegans who eat lower levels of ALA do have a higher conversion rate than do omnivores. So to some degree, the body compensates for lower input of ALA.

[https://www.vitalchoice.com/article/fish-avoiders-have-more-omega-3s-than-expected](https://www.vitalchoice.com/article/fish-avoiders-have-more-omega-3s-than-expected)

there are references to the studies at the bottom of the article. So while it's well known that vegetarians ingest far lower levels of the parent omega-3 fat, and often are urged to supplement, especially when pregnant or nursing, this article just proves that the human body has a feedback loop with regard to DHA, and can change the amount that it derives on an "as needed" basis. If this weren't true, infants would not be able to push so much DHA into their brains and eyes when they needed it. The reduction of the conversion rate, I deduce, is designed, (necessary probably, as well as efficient!) and not the result of environmental insults always present in man's environment since prehistoric times.

While I am not at all a vegetarian, I not only think I don't risk low levels of DHA, but believe I probably have a higher level in my body than 95% of the world population due to the freshness and nutrient content of my food, most of which I raise on tested and properly mineralized soil.

On your last point about PUFA research being done using unhealthy oils, yes, that's why I've found that almost all PUFA research is worthless. Whether they are testing saturated vs unsaturated fats, or the effects of PUFA on heart health, it's all a dead end if they are using the commercially marketed "rat foods" as their PUFA while the savvy members of our society wouldn't allow anything like that to reach our tables.

On Michael Crawford, I've read some of his work. He emphasizes the importance of DHA, in both the development of more powerful brains in evolution of out species, and in maintenance of central nervous system health in modern times with environmental threats. I haven't read enough of his work to determine if he knows how much DHA can actually be used in the optimum case, but I get the impression that his slant is to imply that "more is always better", a "fish diet is always better" without regard to how much DHA can actually be used at any given moment. It's easy to point out that a certain diet can provide far more preformed DHA than another diet. It's a totally different thing however to come up with a minimum amount of DHA that is sufficient to allow no threat to health. And all I would say here is, without knowing what the minimal need for DHA is, who is to say that, say, a fish diet is ever even important, except to a person, or peoples, who are chronically experience a shortage of DHA.

Crawford is more intent it seems, on building a theory on why Homo Sapiens has a large, smart brain. He gives credit to early man's ingestion of marine animals and how this gradually allowed the development of more and more grey matter. I don't want to take on his theory to disprove it, but I wonder why he sees fish as the only source of preformed DHA, when

1.  Isn't the presence of DHA (via ALA) only high in cold-water fish, yet Homo sapiens developed in Africa, and who knows how near the coast?

2.  Doesn't DHA exist in high levels in all brain and eye tissue, and thus is ingested preferentially by carnivores that nonetheless seem to maintain distinctly lower levels of intelligence than Homo sapiens in spite of their DHA rich diet?

I can't find Crawford addressing these subjects in his writings. Do you know if he talks about these aspects of DHA consumption that would seem to raise questions about his theory?

I don't understand your statement about "...low light arctic environment with uncoupled mito haplotype v the requirement in the tropics with closely coupled mito....". Where can I learn what that is about?

Thanks!

### Linz — 2018-11-26T21:45:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256647>

Good evening Glenn,
Apologies for my slow response. A busy weekend and then I went back to the Bazan blog!

My comment about the vitamin A and D in oily fish was probably confusing without enough context. I really meant to say I think some of those who think they are adversely affected by too much DHA may in fact be suffering from vitamin toxicity. If you have read any of the most recent blogs on melanopsin it has recently been discovered that retinol is released from melanopsin by excessive blue light and nnEMFs, and once released in causes circadian havoc. It is not the high DHA levels that unbalance the vitamins. The blue light does this but it also causes breakdown of DHA. It is worth looking at the blogs on vitamin D too, as A and D need to be coupled. My understanding is that Vit A is not really in short supply, more that it is out of control and supplementing in a poor environment would not be safe. Vitamin D levels are usually low now but again 'supplementation' has to come from sunlight to work properly.

The Bazan effect blog answers quite a few of the questions you have asked me far more eloquently than I will ever do but I'll try. The long loop processes dietary DHA via the liver so long as the liver is functioning well (not leptin resistant). The short loop recycles DHA from retinal pigmented epithelium to photoreceptors and vv so this DHA should not be lost in an ideal environment. Dietary DHA must be in SN-2 position (fish/shellfish etc) but the recycled DHA in the short loop maintains its SN 2 configuration. This is important as SN 2 DHA is para-magnetic so it is drawn to mitochondrial magnetic fields. Hence, drawn to brain and eye tissue.
This is relevant to my mitochondrial haplotype statement (see end of Bazan blog for some more information) which relates to brown fat function - people who need to generate heat in cold climates can uncouple mito respiration using uncoupling proteins to generate free heat. These people generally need to eat more fish to process DHA via the long loop as their short loop is not so effective in low light that goes with cold areas. In the tropics there is less need to generate heat and the more intense light helps the short loop (which also conserves/re-cycles DHA in a way that the long loop can't). If you Google Jack Kruse and uncoupling proteins, mitochondrial function, haplotype etc many relevant blogs should come up. Each individual has their own haplotype depending on where their maternal line came from and thus, differing ability to couple/uncouple so different priorities in the Bazan effect function.

I'm running out of time just now but the brain gut series of blogs goes into the marine influence on human brain and body development in depth and the reasons why carnivore brains didn't develop the same way.

I don't know much about minimum requirements for DHA but the Bazan effect illustrates how it can be recycled and why it is under assault at the moment I hope I haven't just caused more confusion.

### Glenn A — 2018-11-29T03:50:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-256891>

Thanks, Linz --

Don't worry. I didn't interpret you as saying that high levels of DHA can cause problems. And I can accept your claim that blue light is a culprit in unbalancing vitamins.

Do you find that yourself or others are over supplementing with vitamin A? Have you experienced a reaction from excess vitamin A? I do supplement with vitamin A. I agree with you that ideal levels of D can be attained from sun exposure. Being probably a poor converter of b-carotene, I take an A supplement, and have raised it recently to try to maximize eye health as I age. Do you see a problem with this?

On Bazan, I've read only Jack's Reality #4. I get most of it. And what you say about the haplotype determining the need for exogenous DHA input via the liver, where cold climate history and present situations may require more DHA. I see this, and have no problem with the explanation.

Where I leave this for now, is that you, Jack and I all agree that conditions determine the amount of DHA that needs to be provided. Where I'm taking a step to the side though is, instead of jumping on the cold-water fishing boat to join others seeking to raise DHA intake to some unspecified level, just to be sure, I am pausing to ask the question: "Does anyone have an idea on how much DHA intake is needed daily, even as a maximum conceivable that would give us the optimum health, especially in eyes and brain, that JK recommends?"

Does this sound reasonable, which suggests an adult needs ~ 1.6 grams of parent omega-3 daily (male) and 1.1 grams (female)?

[https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/Omega3FattyAcids-HealthProfessional/](https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/Omega3FattyAcids-HealthProfessional/)

Maybe close to .6 - 1.0 gram of DHA could derive then?

Mentioned in the article is the competition for desaturation enzymes between parent o-6 and o-3, as well as competition between their derivatives, one with the other (AA with EPA, etc.). It just seems reasonable that one can take too much of either of these essential oils such that availability of the other's derivatives (ALA, DHA, etc.) can be compromised. This is usually pointed out with respect to over indulgence on omega-6 oils, but also pertains to excessive intake of omega-3 as was mentioned above by myself and JanSz. Patricia and Edward Kane have done a lot of research on this in a clinical setting.

But actually as important as intake of omega-3, and protecting it from damage so that DHA may be derived, is the need to simultaneously protect omega-6 from oxidation which can happen from any combination of time spent in heat, light, or atmosphere. Also, processing of oils can produce trans fats which prevent the derivation of DHA from parent omega-3 rich foods. But further, the presence of trans fats in the body and also intentional hydrogenation of oils via commercial processes will destroy the oil-soluble substances like vitamins D and E, beta-carotene, lutein and ziaxanthin. So then, there go anti-oxidants that were with oils in their native state, but may be destroyed by oxidation or intentionally removed in a process to refine a substance such as DHA before placing it in a capsule.

There is a study that shows that when parent o-6 levels are low, lutein and ziaxanthin are protective from age related macular degeneration (AMD), however, when intake of parent o-6 is high, _raising_ the intake of lutein and ziaxanthin will cause a net _increas_e in the risk of developing AMD -- suggesting a possible inflammatory response. Yikes.

I didn't find a direct link to the above study, but if you wish to view more on this (and also 2 articles by Patricia Kane) you may "look inside" the volume entitled _Food and Nutrients in Disease Management_ found here on Amazon: (see page 8 in the book)

[https://www.amazon.com/Nutrients-Disease-Management-Ingrid-Kohlstadt/dp/1420067621/](https://www.amazon.com/Nutrients-Disease-Management-Ingrid-Kohlstadt/dp/1420067621/)

So I don't see this as an easy puzzle to solve, but I think it's worth considering that overdoses are not necessarily a solution, and that when at all possible, using a Paleolithic diet is a good starting point, and may be safer than taking supplements without regard to overdose possibilities.

I certainly see no problem with obtaining PUFA from fish, unless it is that oxidation of ALA within a fish that is frozen and thawed for resale is even greater than within capsules. Modern man really has hardly any way to duplicate the viability of omega-3 that was obtainable by hunter-gatherers. Wild animals seem to know to eat eyes and brain immediately. There must be a reason for this. I'm thinking "fresh" should be in front of every item mentioned in a list of Paleo foods.

Glenn, You really need to read the recent patreon blogs on melanopsin soon if you are supplementing vitamin A. I don't supplement with anything except some turmeric/curcumin and seaweeds in cooking plus a little liver on regular occasions. I had some nasty auto-immune problems that most certainly involved vitamin A toxicity but not from food supplements. Please don't just accept my 'claim' about light and vitamins, those blogs and the references in them are eye opening.

I'm staying on the fishing boat (but maybe not the cold water boat in summer) because extra seafood made a huge difference to me after about 2 years using paleo/scd with no processed food at all, no processed oils (except evoo) and mostly local meat and vegetables - Skin lost 10 years, teeth regained whiteness and many little problems I hadn't even recognised disappeared. Epipaleo recommends: 1. Shellfish 2. Crustaceans 3. Fish. Not the highest levels of DHA first. I don't know know about max/min levels of DHA/ALA etc or conversion percentages and haven't looked into them in any depth because my priority has been to optimise my environment as this determines how they are incorporated into membranes for proper function and the actual levels will change all the time. I just try not to do anything too extreme or out of season.

Jan - perhaps blood levels of DHA are high when too much is being consumesd in a warm summer environment or by someone with a haplotype that does not match their environment so it is not needed for membrane fluidity? When sick, circadian mechanisms are usually (always?) broken so it is unlikely lipid/membrane function will be optimal

I read some of the Kane's work a long time ago but too much is based on isolated lipids and their various combinations for my reasoning. Lipids in seafood (and other whole foods) come with protective nutrients- selenium, iodine etc.

### Glenn A — 2018-12-13T04:18:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-258031>

> [JackieNewZealand said:](/goto/post?id=258005)
>
> There seem to be a LOT of people who don't eat seafood and eat ONLY meat. They are probably getting small amounts of DHA from the meat, but not as much as oysters. So wonder how much we really need ... perhaps not huge amounts. Some people have been doing the carnivore diet for 10 to 20 years - see [http://meatheals.com/](http://meatheals.com/) website or
> hear this doctor talk about only eating meat - he raises some little known stuff about the hazards of dietary fiber etc:
>

Thanks, Jackie. Good points!

Mostly just in the last 4 months I've become aware of a lot of discussion and clinical evidence on the value of a carnivore diet. People seem driven to it especially when they suffer from IBS, Crohn's, diverticulosis, ulcerative colitis, etc. as these diseases seem to be helped by a pure-meat diet, when no other diet brings relief from their symptoms. And from what I've read, some people stick to such a diet for years. Some of them also return to a diet involving considerable plant material after a number of years and are finally able to escape a recurrence of their gut issues.

I'm convinced that a carnivore diet can maintain health if quality and variety (eat the whole animal) and freshness are considered.

So I totally share your wonder about "How much we really need" of DHA. My link to a Brian Peskin article above, plus Jack's article cited above both make the point that we don't need much DHA compared to how much we have on board already in the nervous system. Elsewhere I've pointed out that our bodies are totally capable of converting parent omega-3, alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), to DHA. This is done at an obviously high rate in infancy, and at a much lower rate in adulthood, and the rate varies depending on diet (vegan vs omnivorous). This variability based on age and diet tells me that our bodies are in control and we don't need to worry about it. There's obviously a feedback loop in effect that takes care of it. Else our infant rate of conversion would be the same as the adult rate. But the rate is not fixed. Fish oil sales material will never tell you this. They always sell fisn oil by saying the human body has "a very low conversion rate of 1 - 3 %)". Well, I say, that's because that's all that is needed to be converted from the parent to the DHA derivative. If more is needed, and omega-3 is on board, the conversion rate will be higher.

I wrote more on the conversion of ALA to DHA on this post:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...he-conversion-rates-for-omega-3-to-dha.22578/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/whats-are-the-conversion-rates-for-omega-3-to-dha.22578/)

Thanks for the Joe Rogan YouTube. It makes the point about the carnivore diet very well.

### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-13T05:06:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-258034>

Thank you ... very interesting and I will look at the ALA to DHA post.

One thing that confuses me is a close friend who had severe rheumatoid arthritis for almost 8 years, became vegan (lots of green drinks) and found his pain completely went away. He thought he was "healed" so decided to dive into his favorite food - RIB EYE STEAK - the next day he had a terrible flare up of pain. I find this confusing! Must be some problem with mixing foods? Not sure.

I've started on a modified carnivore and am giving it a 6 week trial ... so far, so good. But the other thing is, if everyone wanted to go carnivore, not sure it would be feasible for the planet and would probably increase the cost of meat to sky high levels. Paradox is one wants to tell people but I don't want everyone buying the short supply of tender liver treats that are currently only sporadically available where i am.

### Glenn A — 2018-12-13T05:51:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-258037>

Your first paragraph makes sense to me. RA is a auto-immune condition, so leaky gut is suspect. Going vegan implies a high fiber diet. Normally, high fiber is good. It's only the small percent of people who have those large intestine disorders mentioned above that have their condition aggravated by fiber. Otherwise, fiber is a valuable part of a diet. So lots of fiber, assuming it's fresh, organic, etc. tends to breed the bacteria that produce the milieu (enzymes, vitamins, motility, gut/brain communications) that allows healing of leaky gut. This healthy gut might relieve one of rheumatoid arthritis symptoms!

Any drastic change in diet may cause a problem in the gut. By "dive in" I'm thinking he might have eaten at least an 8 oz. steak. From my research, even people eating meat regularly can drive themselves to ill health by eating more than 3-4 oz. of protein at one time, as the gut only produces so much of the enzymes needed to break down the protein. Any excess not absorbed is going to ferment in the colon. This won't be comfortable. So it's not the mixing of meat with veggies that's the problem that I can see.

So that's something to keep in mind when you go carnivore: Spread your protein out into 2 or 3 meals and don't ever eat more than about 3 oz. of meat in a meal if you can avoid it. Eating a 10 oz. steak is not good for anyone.

Well, I don't worry about converting an excess of people to carnivore. For one thing, far too many are addicted to cheap, near-worthless grains! But if humanity stopped doing feed-lots and just grazed animals on what used to be (in USA, Argentina, etc.) the vast range land in the heartland, it woudn't be so difficult to get some really good food into the system again! We need to return to grass and stop growing all this grain.

Good luck on the carnivore diet! Yes, give it at least a month! That's what the practitioners suggest to give it a fair chance.

### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-13T06:16:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-258038>

Too many people eating super high fiber and still feeling sick and tired. Have you seen this book "The Fiber Menace" ... it makes some very logical points and have a glance at the customer reviews:

[https://www.amazon.com/Fiber-Menace...d=1544674412&sr=8-1&keywords=the+fiber+menace](https://www.amazon.com/Fiber-Menace-Constipation-Hemorrhoids-Ulcerative/dp/0970679645/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1544674412&sr=8-1&keywords=the+fiber+menace)

### Glenn A — 2018-12-13T12:50:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-regarding-fb-page-referenced-in-all-things-seafood-for-the-skeptic.22537/post-258042>

Yes, I researched, though didn't read, _The Fiber Menace_. I've read many reviews and watched a half dozen YouTube presentations selling the ideas in the book.

I feel as I said before: Most people thrive on fresh vegetable fiber. Fiber as sold as a supplement or mentioned in a recommendation for "more fiber" is often manufactured and comes from grain and seeds. I won't say that there's any value to consuming this type of fiber, especially when all fruits and vegetables contain fiber and at the same time provide a great range of other nutrients including enzymes and vitamins. From my reading of parts of Fiber Menance, he is looking at people who have horrible nutrition and take a fiber supplement that often causes problems, and then switch to his diet and thrive by comparison.

What I'll mention without going too far into this, is that you will have a very different gut microbiome when you switch to a carnivore diet. Carnivorous animals don't live more than 30 years. Omnivores and vegetarians live over 100 years in some species, including humans (though not the Innuit and Massai). I suggest reading about the importance of the culture of large intestinal microbes in human health. Variety is said to be very important. I think restricting a diet to either mostly meats, or mostly refined carbohydrates spells trouble for one's health. It is often said that our gut's microbiology constitutes 80% of our immune system. _The Fiber Menace_ doesn't spend time discussing this and explaining how a carnivore diet will maintain a microbial mix that will keep the gut and the whole person in a state of strong immunity.

Jack Kruse has a lot to say about maintaining gut health also. His paleolithic diet stresses many things, but one is keeping the omega-6/omega-3 ratio relatively low, like below 4/1. You are not going to accomplish this on a carnivore diet, though you might come close if you eat only wild game that often has about a 4/1 ratio. Free range beef has a ratio more like 5/1 or 6/1. Not bad. But CAFO meat products, besides having antibiotics and other toxins in them, are going to have an even higher percent of omega-6.

So while I say that going on a carnivore diet is fine for a period of time as you suggest doing, for my own health, as long as I have a healthy gut that loves fiber from fresh fruits and vegetables, I couldn't be tempted to pass up all the fresh vitamins, minerals enzymes and phytonutrients that I get from vegetation. When you go on a carnivore diet, be sure you are getting enough healthy fats too. Even on a carnivore diet, your protein intake should probably not be more than 40% of your calories. Very high protein diets (like if you ate nothing but wild rabbit or caribou) will kill you quickly:

[http://discovermagazine.com/2004/oct/inuit-paradox](http://discovermagazine.com/2004/oct/inuit-paradox)

I am able to follow a paleolithic diet by raising my own vegetables and consuming mostly, by weight, vegetables. The meats I buy are free-range or organic, but they have been butchered and frozen without my supervision. How much nutrition is still in the meat by the time I thaw and eat it? It certainly isn't paleolithic if I consider the delay from the kill to presentation on the plate. This, and the change in my gut bacteria and the resultant effect on my immune system, plus the short lifespan of studied carnivores are the reasons I don't see a reason, personally, to part from an omnivore diet.

# Thread: Heat stress, Heat shock proteins & Mitochondria news again...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-stress-heat-shock-proteins-mitochondria-news-again.22541/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2018-11-23T00:23:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-stress-heat-shock-proteins-mitochondria-news-again.22541/post-256292>

More about heat-shock proteins and heat stress with the mitochondria...

"Defects that occur in the biogenesis or function of the mitochondria can cause serious neurological or cardiovascular disease. To maintain proper function, mitochondria rely on around 1,000 different proteins that are imported from the cellular fluid or cytosol.

Ribosomes within the cytosol not only produce proteins for the mitochondria but also precursor proteins, which are escorted from the ribosomes to the mitochondria by chaperone proteins. Since the interior components of these chaperones increases in response to heat stress, these molecules are also referred to as heat shock proteins (Hsps). One Hsps called Hsp70 is particularly important for carrying the precursors into mitochondria."

[https://www.news-medical.net/news/20181122/Researchers-discover-new-mitochondrial-mechanism.aspx](https://www.news-medical.net/news/20181122/Researchers-discover-new-mitochondrial-mechanism.aspx)

After exactly 20 seconds of a cold shower, all neuropathy ceases... so, for all of you out there who don't feel like doing the whole ice bath thing, just one crappy little minute or 2 or a cold shower will help you... I would also recommend rubbing epsom salts and baking soda all over your body for a few minutes - do some yoga head stands against the shower wall and then as your body heats up, you will actually \*desire\* a cold shower... you can also just use a garden hose after baking in the sun - doing the garden hose thing a few times a day resulted in 10 lbs of weight loss over a period on one month... the more screwed up you are and your environment is, the more of the cold you must "embrace" - but, if you're a total wimp, even a wimp can do 1 minute of a cold shower and 30 seconds of a garden hose... baby steps

A couple of summers ago, when I was hacking the ol' nicotine gum, I found that just sticking my face in a tub of ice water enabled me to use the nicotine gum and killed the nausea... better to do something than nothing...
